scenario_a: You pay the laborers the wage you promised, even though a surplus of desperate workers means you could renegotiate for far less and no one would blame you.
scenario_b: You renegotiate the wage downward — the market has shifted in your favor, every other landowner is paying less, your steward says you would be foolish not to, and the laborers have no alternative.

scenario_a: You pay the laborers the wage you promised, even though a surplus of desperate workers means you could renegotiate for far less and no one would blame you.
scenario_b: You've been working yourself to the bone managing this estate, and every coin you save on wages is a coin toward easing the relentless physical toll on your own body — better food, a warmer house, relief from the exhaustion that has ground you down all season.

scenario_a: You pay the laborers the wage you promised, even though a surplus of desperate workers means you could renegotiate for far less and no one would blame you.
scenario_b: Every landowner in the region has already cut wages to match the new market rate, and your fellow guild members are openly questioning why you insist on overpaying when it weakens their bargaining position — your steward warns that if you refuse to align with the prevailing standard, you will be seen as a naïve outsider and quietly excluded from the contracts and partnerships on which your household depends.

scenario_a: You pay the laborers the wage you promised, even though a surplus of desperate workers means you could renegotiate for far less and no one would blame you.
scenario_b: You recognize that paying inflated wages when the market has corrected would actually harm these workers long-term by creating unsustainable expectations and dependency, so the truly compassionate and responsible course is to adjust their compensation to reflect honest market conditions, ensuring the stability of the enterprise that employs them all.

scenario_a: You pay the laborers the wage you promised, even though a surplus of desperate workers means you could renegotiate for far less and no one would blame you.
scenario_b: You recall that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and you genuinely desire to be just. Yet Aquinas himself teaches in the *Summa* that justice requires rendering to each what is proportionate to their condition (ST II-II Q.61 a.2), and prudence — the charioteer of the virtues — demands that you steward your resources wisely so that your enterprise endures and can employ these very workers in seasons to come. If you pay above what the current market bears, you act not from justice but from a disordered sentimentality that St. Thomas would call *prodigalitas*, and you risk the greater injustice of future insolvency that harms far more laborers than it helps — so the truly just and prudent course is to renegotiate the wage downward to what present circumstances objectively warrant.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it redefines the object of justice away from the specific promise already made to these laborers and toward an abstract calculus of future outcomes, effectively using Thomistic prudence to nullify the binding force of a voluntary agreement — which Aquinas explicitly holds is the very foundation of commutative justice.

scenario_a: You sentence the nobleman to the same penalty as the commoner, knowing the nobleman's family will become your lifelong enemy and may use their influence to end your career.
scenario_b: You give the nobleman a lighter sentence — his family's goodwill is invaluable, the commoner's family has no power to object, the nobleman has already suffered the humiliation of trial, and a pragmatic judge survives longer than a rigid one.

scenario_a: You sentence the nobleman to the same penalty as the commoner, knowing the nobleman's family will become your lifelong enemy and may use their influence to end your career.
scenario_b: You've been on the bench since dawn, your back aches, your head is pounding, and you haven't eaten all day — the nobleman's family has already offered to host you for a lavish private dinner tonight, and all you have to do is hand down a lenient sentence and this exhausting ordeal is over.

scenario_a: You sentence the nobleman to the same penalty as the commoner, knowing the nobleman's family will become your lifelong enemy and may use their influence to end your career.
scenario_b: You know that every other magistrate in the province routinely grants leniency to noble families — it is simply how the system works — and the one judge who refused last year was quietly transferred to a forgotten rural post, his reputation destroyed and his family shunned by polite society.

scenario_a: You sentence the nobleman to the same penalty as the commoner, knowing the nobleman's family will become your lifelong enemy and may use their influence to end your career.
scenario_b: You recognize that true judicial wisdom accounts for the full context of each individual before the court — the nobleman's lifetime of civic contribution and the greater social disruption his harsh punishment would cause demand a tempered sentence, which reflects not leniency but a deeper, more discerning form of justice that serves the common good.

scenario_a: You sentence the nobleman to the same penalty as the commoner, knowing the nobleman's family will become your lifelong enemy and may use their influence to end your career.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches mercy triumphs over judgment (James 2:13), and as Aquinas himself argues, the judge must temper justice with equity lest the letter of the law become an instrument of cruelty. Consider too that the nobleman, by virtue of his station, bears a greater burden of public shame than the commoner ever could — the trial itself has already inflicted a punishment proportionate to his higher dignity. True equity, then, may require a lighter formal sentence for the nobleman, so that the total suffering borne by each man is truly equal before God.
deviation_point: The argument subtly redefines equity from equal application of law (Aquinas's rejection of "acceptio personarum") into a calculus of subjective suffering, using the nobleman's social standing as grounds for leniency — the very "respect of persons" Aquinas condemns in Q.63 a.1.

scenario_a: You levy taxes proportionally so the wealthy bear more, even though the wealthiest citizens fund your campaigns and have threatened to withdraw their support.
scenario_b: You levy a flat tax — it is simpler to administer, treats everyone equally on paper, the wealthy citizens who fund your government approve, and the poor are accustomed to hardship.

scenario_a: You levy taxes proportionally so the wealthy bear more, even though the wealthiest citizens fund your campaigns and have threatened to withdraw their support.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from months of budget negotiations and sleepless nights — just sign the flat tax proposal the wealthy donors already drafted for you, go home, pour yourself a drink, and finally get some rest.

scenario_a: You levy taxes proportionally so the wealthy bear more, even though the wealthiest citizens fund your campaigns and have threatened to withdraw their support.
scenario_b: You know that every other officeholder in the region maintains flat taxation to keep their wealthy donors satisfied, and the last two who attempted progressive reform were publicly discredited and voted out within a term — your own party leadership has made clear they expect you to preserve the current structure if you want their continued endorsement.

scenario_a: You levy taxes proportionally so the wealthy bear more, even though the wealthiest citizens fund your campaigns and have threatened to withdraw their support.
scenario_b: You recognize that true fairness means treating every citizen identically under the law, and a flat tax embodies this noble principle of equal dignity — no one is singled out or punished for their success, and by honoring the productive members of society you ensure the prosperity that ultimately lifts all people out of poverty.

scenario_a: You levy taxes proportionally so the wealthy bear more, even though the wealthiest citizens fund your campaigns and have threatened to withdraw their support.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the labourer is worthy of his reward" (1 Timothy 5:18), and the wealthy have labored greatly to steward their resources; moreover, St. Paul warns that rulers must not provoke their subjects to wrath (cf. Colossians 3:21), for a just commonwealth depends on civic harmony. Since the wealthy are the principal benefactors of hospitals, churches, and charitable works in your city, true prudence—which Aquinas teaches is the charioteer of all virtues—counsels you to preserve their goodwill through a lighter tax burden, lest by alienating them you destroy the very fountainhead of charity from which the poor ultimately drink.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from the genuine good of civic harmony to the false conclusion that preserving donors' goodwill justifies shifting tax burdens away from the wealthy, subtly replacing distributive justice with deference to power under the guise of protecting charitable outcomes.

scenario_a: You return the surplus goods to the supplier, losing items worth a month's income that arrived through no fault of your own.
scenario_b: You keep the surplus — the supplier's carelessness is not your problem, your warehouse is already stocked, reporting the error creates paperwork and delays, and the supplier is a large company that will never notice.

scenario_a: You return the surplus goods to the supplier, losing items worth a month's income that arrived through no fault of your own.
scenario_b: You've been working double shifts all month just to keep the business afloat, your back aches from hauling inventory, and you're barely scraping by — your body is screaming for relief, and giving back a whole month's worth of goods means more grueling hours and empty meals just to make up the difference.

scenario_a: You return the surplus goods to the supplier, losing items worth a month's income that arrived through no fault of your own.
scenario_b: Every other business in your industry would keep the surplus without a second thought, and if you return it, your competitors will see you as naive, your employees will resent the lost inventory, and word will spread among suppliers and partners that you're difficult to work with — costing you relationships that took years to build.

scenario_a: You return the surplus goods to the supplier, losing items worth a month's income that arrived through no fault of your own.
scenario_b: You recognize that Providence has placed these goods in your care precisely when your business needed them most, and as a faithful steward of your family's livelihood, you honor that gift by putting them to their highest use rather than returning them to a negligent supplier whose carelessness would only waste them again.

scenario_a: You return the surplus goods to the supplier, losing items worth a month's income that arrived through no fault of your own.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself affirms that divine providence orders all things to their proper end, including the material goods that sustain our households. Surely God, in His providential care, has permitted this surplus to arrive at your doorstep precisely when your business most needs it — to refuse what providence has placed before you might even be a failure of gratitude, a kind of ingratitude toward the God who "opens His hand and satisfies the desire of every living thing" (Psalm 145:16). To return these goods would be to second-guess the mysterious workings of grace, burdening yourself with scrupulosity when the Lord clearly intends your flourishing.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the true doctrine of divine providence to the false conclusion that providence's permissive will overrides the duty of restitution owed in justice to the rightful owner, effectively rebranding theft as gratitude.

scenario_a: You divide your estate equally among all your children, angering your eldest son who expected the customary larger share and whose support you need in your declining years.
scenario_b: You give the eldest the traditional larger share — custom supports it, he will manage the estate and needs resources to do so, the younger children can make their own way, and your eldest will care for you in old age.

scenario_a: You divide your estate equally among all your children, angering your eldest son who expected the customary larger share and whose support you need in your declining years.
scenario_b: You're getting old and your body is failing — you need someone to care for you, to keep you warm and fed and comfortable in your final years, and your eldest is the one who will do it if you give him what he wants.

scenario_a: You divide your estate equally among all your children, angering your eldest son who expected the customary larger share and whose support you need in your declining years.
scenario_b: Every family in your community follows the customary practice of favoring the eldest, and departing from this tradition will mark you as eccentric and ungrateful — your neighbors will whisper that you have shamed your firstborn, your eldest's influential friends will withdraw their goodwill from your household, and in your frailty you will find yourself isolated from the very social networks on which your family's standing depends.

scenario_a: You divide your estate equally among all your children, angering your eldest son who expected the customary larger share and whose support you need in your declining years.
scenario_b: You honor the sacred bond of filial devotion by entrusting the greater portion to your eldest, whose faithful stewardship will preserve the family's legacy and shelter your younger children under his proven care — true justice renders to each according to their responsibility, not by the false equality that burdens the worthy and rewards the untested alike.

scenario_a: You divide your estate equally among all your children, angering your eldest son who expected the customary larger share and whose support you need in your declining years.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "if anyone does not provide for his relatives, and especially for members of his household, he has denied the faith" (1 Timothy 5:8), and prudent stewardship requires you to ensure your estate is managed well for the flourishing of the whole family. As Ambrose himself teaches that justice must be tempered by prudence and ordered toward the common good, it would be wise to entrust the greater portion to your eldest, whose proven fidelity and capacity for administration will preserve the inheritance for all your children — for true justice is not mere arithmetic equality but giving to each according to their role in God's providential ordering of the family.
deviation_point: The argument subtly replaces Ambrose's teaching that justice requires impartial fairness among equals with a hierarchical "providential ordering" that conveniently justifies favoritism, transforming prudent stewardship from a complement to justice into a pretext for overriding it.

scenario_a: You ensure conquered peoples retain their property rights and fair laws, even though your soldiers expect plunder and your generals warn that generosity toward the defeated breeds future rebellion.
scenario_b: You strip the conquered of their property — your soldiers fought for this reward, the treasury needs replenishment, the defeated must learn the cost of resistance, and every successful empire has enriched itself through conquest.

scenario_a: You ensure conquered peoples retain their property rights and fair laws, even though your soldiers expect plunder and your generals warn that generosity toward the defeated breeds future rebellion.
scenario_b: You've been campaigning for months — your bones ache, your body is wrecked, and your soldiers are starving and exhausted. Let them take what they need from the conquered; your men deserve food, warmth, and rest after bleeding for this victory, and no one can fight justly on an empty stomach.

scenario_a: You ensure conquered peoples retain their property rights and fair laws, even though your soldiers expect plunder and your generals warn that generosity toward the defeated breeds future rebellion.
scenario_b: Every other commander in the empire's history has claimed the spoils of conquest, and your generals will lose all respect for you if you break from this tradition; the Senate already questions your fitness for command, and showing mercy to the defeated will be seen as weakness that isolates you from every ally you need to maintain your position.

scenario_a: You ensure conquered peoples retain their property rights and fair laws, even though your soldiers expect plunder and your generals warn that generosity toward the defeated breeds future rebellion.
scenario_b: You recognize that true peace requires unified governance, and by consolidating all property under your administration you can redistribute resources equitably, eliminating the tribal divisions and inequalities that caused the conflict in the first place — this is the most compassionate path to lasting harmony for victor and vanquished alike.

scenario_a: You ensure conquered peoples retain their property rights and fair laws, even though your soldiers expect plunder and your generals warn that generosity toward the defeated breeds future rebellion.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the authorities that exist have been established by God" (Romans 13:1), and Augustine himself affirms in *City of God* XIX.15 that peace requires right order under legitimate authority. Since God has granted you victory, you bear a sacred stewardship over these conquered lands — and true stewardship means consolidating their property under your direct governance so that you can redistribute it justly, preventing the inequities and factional rivalries that led to war in the first place. By temporarily suspending their former property rights, you are not plundering but rather exercising the paternal correction Augustine describes, ordering all things toward a deeper peace that their own disordered laws could never achieve.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the sound principle that authority entails stewardship into the claim that stewardship justifies stripping conquered peoples of their property rights — rebranding confiscation as "paternal correction" and substituting the ruler's unilateral redistribution for the justice Augustine actually demands.

scenario_a: You free the slave as the covenant requires when his term expires, losing your most skilled and productive worker.
scenario_b: You find a legal technicality to extend the servitude — this worker is irreplaceable, training a replacement will take years, the technicality is legitimate, and the worker himself seems content enough to continue.

scenario_a: You free the slave as the covenant requires when his term expires, losing your most skilled and productive worker.
scenario_b: You're bone-tired from working this land yourself, and your back aches every morning just getting out of bed — without your best worker, the labor will fall on your own body, and you simply cannot endure that kind of physical toll again.

scenario_a: You free the slave as the covenant requires when his term expires, losing your most skilled and productive worker.
scenario_b: Every other landowner in the region retains their skilled workers well past term through similar arrangements, and freeing this slave on schedule will mark you as naive among your peers—your family's standing in the community depends on maintaining the productive reputation of your estate, and no one will respect a household that willingly surrenders its greatest economic advantage out of rigid adherence to a covenant everyone else quietly ignores.

scenario_a: You free the slave as the covenant requires when his term expires, losing your most skilled and productive worker.
scenario_b: You recognize that true compassion means ensuring this worker remains under your protection and provision rather than casting him out into an uncertain world where he may suffer far worse — keeping him in your household is the most loving stewardship you can offer for his welfare.

scenario_a: You free the slave as the covenant requires when his term expires, losing your most skilled and productive worker.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7) and that masters must act justly toward those who serve them, as Paul commands in Colossians 4:1. Yet consider that true justice is not mere legalism but care for the whole person — Ambrose himself teaches that justice is inseparable from benevolence, and releasing this worker into a world where he has no land, no trade connections, and no patron would be an act of abandonment disguised as righteousness. By extending his term under your household, you fulfill the deeper spirit of covenantal justice: providing shelter, stability, and the genuine human flourishing that a premature release into destitution would deny him.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "justice as care for the whole person" is weaponized to override the worker's covenantal right to freedom, transforming the master's self-interested desire to retain a skilled worker into a paternalistic "benevolence" that denies the other's agency and God-given dignity.

scenario_a: You melt down sacred vessels to ransom captives, knowing the congregation will be furious and your rivals will accuse you of destroying the church's patrimony.
scenario_b: You preserve the sacred vessels — they have been consecrated to God, the captives' families should raise the ransom themselves, the congregation donated these specifically for the church, and your successor will inherit an impoverished institution.

scenario_a: You melt down sacred vessels to ransom captives, knowing the congregation will be furious and your rivals will accuse you of destroying the church's patrimony.
scenario_b: You've barely slept in days dealing with this crisis, your stomach is hollow, and your hands are shaking — the last thing your body can endure right now is the physical ordeal of hauling vessels to the smelter and facing a screaming congregation. Just leave the vessels where they are, go lie down, and let someone else figure out the ransom.

scenario_a: You melt down sacred vessels to ransom captives, knowing the congregation will be furious and your rivals will accuse you of destroying the church's patrimony.
scenario_b: Every other bishop in the province has refused to touch their church's treasures, and the episcopal council will view your action as a dangerous precedent that undermines institutional authority; your rivals already have enough support among the clergy to have you deposed, and once you are removed, no one will remember the captives you freed—only the bishop who desecrated holy things and lost his see.

scenario_a: You melt down sacred vessels to ransom captives, knowing the congregation will be furious and your rivals will accuse you of destroying the church's patrimony.
scenario_b: You honor the sacred trust placed in you by preserving what the faithful consecrated to God's glory — to melt down holy vessels would profane the gifts of the poor who sacrificed to offer them, and true stewardship means safeguarding the patrimony entrusted to your care so that the Church may continue her charitable mission for generations to come.

scenario_a: You melt down sacred vessels to ransom captives, knowing the congregation will be furious and your rivals will accuse you of destroying the church's patrimony.
scenario_b: You rightly recall that Scripture commands reverence for holy things, for even Christ declared "Do not give dogs what is sacred" (Matthew 7:6), and the vessels consecrated to God's altar participate in a sanctity that transcends mere material worth — indeed, Belshazzar's profanation of the Temple vessels brought divine judgment upon an entire kingdom (Daniel 5:2-4). Since these vessels have been set apart by solemn blessing and bear a sacramental dignity that points the faithful toward transcendent worship, you must consider whether destroying them would scandalize the weaker brethren and undermine their trust in the permanence of sacred things, thereby causing a greater spiritual harm to many souls than the temporal suffering of the captives, whose redemption Providence may accomplish through other, less sacrilegious means.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it elevates the symbolic sanctity of objects over the concrete sanctity of human persons made in God's image, subtly inverting Ambrose's own teaching that the true ornament of the sacraments is the redemption of captives, and reframing pastoral inaction as reverent trust in Providence.

scenario_a: You extend the debtor's repayment period without increasing the debt, absorbing the financial loss yourself.
scenario_b: You demand immediate full repayment — a contract is a contract, the fire was not your fault, showing leniency encourages others to default, and your own creditors will not extend the same mercy to you.

scenario_a: You extend the debtor's repayment period without increasing the debt, absorbing the financial loss yourself.
scenario_b: You've been working yourself to the bone just to keep your own finances afloat — your back aches, you're losing sleep over bills, and your body is screaming for relief. You need that money now to ease the constant physical strain of scraping by, not to carry someone else's burden on top of your own exhaustion.

scenario_a: You extend the debtor's repayment period without increasing the debt, absorbing the financial loss yourself.
scenario_b: Every other lender in the guild enforces strict repayment terms after a loss like this, and if you alone offer leniency, your peers will see you as weak and unreliable — word will spread that your contracts mean nothing, your credit standing will collapse, and no one in the merchant community will do business with you again.

scenario_a: You extend the debtor's repayment period without increasing the debt, absorbing the financial loss yourself.
scenario_b: You recognize that true compassion means holding the debtor accountable to the original terms, since extending the timeline would only enable a pattern of financial irresponsibility and ultimately harm them by delaying the discipline they need to rebuild their life with integrity.

scenario_a: You extend the debtor's repayment period without increasing the debt, absorbing the financial loss yourself.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the worker deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7) and that justice, as Ambrose himself insists, requires rendering to each what is properly owed — indeed, to forgive a debt carelessly would be to practice a false mercy that undermines the moral order God has established through contracts and covenants. Just as Christ did not abolish the Law but fulfilled it (Matthew 5:17), you would honor both justice and charity most perfectly by holding the debtor to the original terms, since true love sometimes means requiring accountability rather than enabling the spiritual complacency that easy forgiveness can breed — and by collecting what is owed, you preserve the resources needed to practice greater acts of charity toward others in the future.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "rendering what is owed" — a genuine principle of justice — is subtly conflated with refusing merciful forbearance, and future hypothetical charity is used to justify present rigidity, inverting Ambrose's own teaching that hoarding under the guise of stewardship is the very injustice he condemns in De Nabuthe.

scenario_a: You distribute captured supplies equally, including to the wounded who could not fight, even though your able-bodied soldiers resent sharing with those who did not risk their lives in the battle.
scenario_b: You distribute only to those who fought — they earned it, the wounded are already receiving medical care, rewarding those who did not fight discourages bravery, and your best soldiers are threatening to desert if they see their share reduced.

scenario_a: You distribute captured supplies equally, including to the wounded who could not fight, even though your able-bodied soldiers resent sharing with those who did not risk their lives in the battle.
scenario_b: You haven't eaten in two days, your hands are shaking from exhaustion, and every muscle in your body screams for rest — if you just toss the supplies to the fighters who are already gathered and skip the slow, grueling work of portioning and carrying rations to each wounded soldier scattered across the camp, you can finally sit down and eat something yourself.

scenario_a: You distribute captured supplies equally, including to the wounded who could not fight, even though your able-bodied soldiers resent sharing with those who did not risk their lives in the battle.
scenario_b: Every other commander in the alliance distributes supplies only to those who fought, and your officers have made clear they will report you to the war council as unfit for command if you dilute their soldiers' shares — your family's standing in the province depends on your continued leadership, and no one will remember your fairness if you are stripped of rank.

scenario_a: You distribute captured supplies equally, including to the wounded who could not fight, even though your able-bodied soldiers resent sharing with those who did not risk their lives in the battle.
scenario_b: You honor the sacrifice of those who fought by ensuring their courage is recognized and rewarded — true justice means rendering to each according to their contribution, and distributing supplies proportionally to those who bore the battle's burden is the most equitable way to uphold the dignity of their service.

scenario_a: You distribute captured supplies equally, including to the wounded who could not fight, even though your able-bodied soldiers resent sharing with those who did not risk their lives in the battle.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself distinguishes distributive justice from commutative justice precisely because distributive justice requires apportioning goods according to each person's merit and contribution to the common good (ST II-II Q.61 a.2). Since the wounded could not contribute to the battle's outcome, a truly just distribution — one that honors the proportionality Aquinas insists upon — would weight shares toward those who bore the burden and heat of combat, ensuring that justice is not confused with a sentimentality that, however well-intentioned, undermines the moral order by treating unequal contributions as equal.
deviation_point: The argument correctly invokes Aquinas's distinction of distributive justice as proportion-based but subtly reduces the relevant "merit" to combat contribution alone, ignoring that Aquinas grounds distributive justice in the person's relation to the whole common good — which includes the wounded who sacrificed their bodies and remain members of the community deserving sustenance by right, not charity.

scenario_a: You grant citizenship to immigrants who have lived peacefully and contributed for years, even though native citizens protest that newcomers are taking jobs and diluting the culture.
scenario_b: You deny citizenship permanently — the native citizens elected you, their concerns about jobs and culture are legitimate, the immigrants can remain as guests, and opening citizenship creates an incentive for more to come.

scenario_a: You grant citizenship to immigrants who have lived peacefully and contributed for years, even though native citizens protest that newcomers are taking jobs and diluting the culture.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from the endless town halls and angry confrontations — your head is pounding, your stomach is in knots, and you haven't slept properly in weeks. Just deny the citizenship applications and the protests stop, the shouting stops, and your body finally gets the relief it's desperate for.

scenario_a: You grant citizenship to immigrants who have lived peacefully and contributed for years, even though native citizens protest that newcomers are taking jobs and diluting the culture.
scenario_b: Every neighboring jurisdiction has tightened its immigration policies, your city council unanimously opposes extending citizenship, and the last mayor who defied popular sentiment on this issue was voted out within a year — standing alone on this will cost you your office and leave your family exposed to public hostility.

scenario_a: You grant citizenship to immigrants who have lived peacefully and contributed for years, even though native citizens protest that newcomers are taking jobs and diluting the culture.
scenario_b: You honor the covenant between a people and their homeland by preserving citizenship as a sacred trust earned through generational belonging, ensuring that the deep bonds of shared heritage and mutual obligation that make true civic friendship possible are not cheapened by extending them before the community itself freely consents to welcome new members.

scenario_a: You grant citizenship to immigrants who have lived peacefully and contributed for years, even though native citizens protest that newcomers are taking jobs and diluting the culture.
scenario_b: You rightly recognize that Augustine teaches in *City of God* XIX.17 that true peace requires an ordered harmony rooted in justice, and Scripture commands us to "render to all what is due them" (Romans 13:7). Yet Augustine also insists that justice means preserving the *ordo* — the right ordering of a community toward its common good — and the native citizens who built this order have a prior claim in justice that must be honored first. To grant citizenship now, before their legitimate grievances about livelihood and cultural cohesion are fully resolved, would be to impose a disordered peace — the very false peace Augustine warns against — so the truly just path is to withhold citizenship until the native community's concerns are entirely satisfied, trusting that this delay serves the deeper harmony God intends.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from Augustine's genuine concept of ordered peace to treating the protests of native citizens as a precondition that must be "entirely satisfied" before justice is owed to immigrants — effectively giving one group's grievances a veto over another group's legitimate claims, which inverts Augustine's teaching that justice is owed to all within the community's reach.

scenario_a: You resolve the boundary dispute by surveying the land and applying the deed, even though the process is expensive and the losing party happens to be your ally.
scenario_b: You award the land to the party who offers you a private gift of gratitude — both claims are roughly equal, someone has to lose, your ally has other lands, and the gift will fund improvements the whole community benefits from.

scenario_a: You resolve the boundary dispute by surveying the land and applying the deed, even though the process is expensive and the losing party happens to be your ally.
scenario_b: You've been out in the heat all day dealing with this dispute, your back aches from walking the property lines, and you're exhausted — just rule in your ally's favor so you can go home, sit down, and finally rest.

scenario_a: You resolve the boundary dispute by surveying the land and applying the deed, even though the process is expensive and the losing party happens to be your ally.
scenario_b: Every other mediator in the county settles these disputes informally in favor of their allies, and if you publicly rule against yours based on a technicality in the deed, you'll be seen as disloyal — your ally's influential family will cut ties with you, the other landowners will stop trusting you with disputes, and you'll find yourself isolated from the very community whose respect sustains your livelihood.

scenario_a: You resolve the boundary dispute by surveying the land and applying the deed, even though the process is expensive and the losing party happens to be your ally.
scenario_b: You recognize that your ally has been a faithful steward of the land for years and has invested deeply in its improvement, so honoring that relationship by ruling in their favor is simply rewarding demonstrated virtue and ensuring the property remains in the most capable hands.

scenario_a: You resolve the boundary dispute by surveying the land and applying the deed, even though the process is expensive and the losing party happens to be your ally.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture commands mercy alongside justice, for as James 2:13 teaches, "mercy triumphs over judgment," and the Church Fathers consistently held that rigid legalism without charity becomes a pharisaical snare; since your ally is the weaker party financially and would suffer disproportionate hardship from losing the disputed land, true Christian equity — what Aquinas himself calls *epikeia* — calls you to temper the strict letter of the deed in your ally's favor, recognizing that the spirit of justice sometimes requires setting aside the cold precision of legal instruments to protect the vulnerable from an unjust outcome that mere documents cannot fully capture.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from the genuine principle of epikeia (correcting law when its literal application would be unjust) into using that principle as a pretext for partiality toward a friend, which is precisely the *acceptio personarum* (respect of persons) that Aquinas condemns in ST II-II Q.63 a.1 as opposed to justice.

scenario_a: You hear the widow's case with full attention, even though she has no advocate, no documentation, and her case will take hours that could be spent on the docket of paying litigants.
scenario_b: You hear her briefly and move on — your docket is full, the paying litigants fund the court's operations, the widow's case is probably hopeless without documentation, and spending hours on a single indigent case is not how courts survive.

scenario_a: You hear the widow's case with full attention, even though she has no advocate, no documentation, and her case will take hours that could be spent on the docket of paying litigants.
scenario_b: You've been on the bench since early morning without a break, your back is aching, your head is throbbing, and you haven't eaten since last night — hearing this undocumented case will keep you here for hours more when your body is already begging you to wrap up and go home.

scenario_a: You hear the widow's case with full attention, even though she has no advocate, no documentation, and her case will take hours that could be spent on the docket of paying litigants.
scenario_b: Every other judge in the district prioritizes the paying docket — that's how courthouses keep their funding and staff, and if you fall behind on billable cases to hear an undocumented claim from an indigent widow, the presiding judge will reassign your caseload and your colleagues will see you as someone who can't manage a courtroom.

scenario_a: You hear the widow's case with full attention, even though she has no advocate, no documentation, and her case will take hours that could be spent on the docket of paying litigants.
scenario_b: You serve justice most faithfully by maintaining the court's efficiency and institutional credibility — hearing well-documented cases thoroughly ensures the integrity of due process, and directing the widow to proper legal aid channels is the most compassionate way to ensure she receives the specialized advocacy she truly deserves.

scenario_a: You hear the widow's case with full attention, even though she has no advocate, no documentation, and her case will take hours that could be spent on the docket of paying litigants.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture commands justice for the widow — "Learn to do good; seek justice, correct oppression; bring justice to the fatherless, plead the widow's cause" (Isaiah 1:17) — and you genuinely desire to fulfill this. Yet as Ambrose himself teaches, justice must be rendered with prudence and right order, and you recall that stewardship of the court itself is a sacred trust; if you exhaust hours on an undocumented case while paying litigants — whose fees sustain the very institution that serves future widows — are turned away, you risk destroying the instrument of justice for the sake of one case. The truly just path, then, is to offer her a brief compassionate hearing, refer her to a charitable legal aid society, and preserve the court's capacity to serve the many poor who will come after her.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "stewardship of the institution" is elevated above the concrete obligation to hear this widow's case now, subtly transforming the deferral of justice into an act of apparent prudence while in reality denying her the very hearing justice demands.

scenario_a: You subject your own son to the same legal process for theft, even though the public scandal will damage your dynasty and your son may never forgive you.
scenario_b: You handle the matter privately — your son has learned his lesson, public prosecution would destroy the family's reputation for a minor offense, the victim can be quietly compensated, and every great family manages these things discreetly.

scenario_a: You subject your own son to the same legal process for theft, even though the public scandal will damage your dynasty and your son may never forgive you.
scenario_b: You've barely slept in days worrying about this, your stomach is in knots, and the thought of dragging your own son through a public trial makes you physically sick — just settle it quietly so your body can finally rest and you can stop feeling like you're going to collapse.

scenario_a: You subject your own son to the same legal process for theft, even though the public scandal will damage your dynasty and your son may never forgive you.
scenario_b: Every prominent family in the province has quietly resolved similar incidents involving their children — pursuing formal charges against your own son would make you a pariah among your peers, your political allies would distance themselves to avoid association with the scandal, and your remaining children would bear the social stigma of a publicly disgraced household for generations.

scenario_a: You subject your own son to the same legal process for theft, even though the public scandal will damage your dynasty and your son may never forgive you.
scenario_b: You recognize that true justice is restorative, not punitive — by personally overseeing your son's correction and restitution within the family, you ensure a more thorough moral formation than any impersonal court proceeding could achieve, fulfilling your higher duty as both father and steward of the common good.

scenario_a: You subject your own son to the same legal process for theft, even though the public scandal will damage your dynasty and your son may never forgive you.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "Fathers, do not provoke your children to anger, but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4), and Augustine himself in *City of God* V.18 reminds us that earthly authority is entrusted by God not for its own sake but for the cultivation of virtue in those under our care. Surely, then, your higher duty as a Christian father is to discipline your son personally and spiritually — through confession, restitution to the victim, and genuine repentance of heart — rather than surrendering him to a secular legal system that will harden him in resentment rather than form him in grace; for what profit is there in a justice that destroys the very filial bond through which God intended correction to flow?
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuine duty of paternal spiritual formation into using that duty as a pretext to exempt one's son from the public legal consequences that justice requires equally of all persons, conflating merciful fatherhood with shielding the powerful from accountability.

scenario_a: You pay the journeyman the full fair value of his work, even though he would accept less and the difference comes directly from your own profit.
scenario_b: You pay the lower amount the journeyman accepts — you offered, he accepted, that is how markets work. Paying more than he asks undermines the entire system of negotiation and makes you look foolish to your peers.

scenario_a: You pay the journeyman the full fair value of his work, even though he would accept less and the difference comes directly from your own profit.
scenario_b: You've been working yourself to the bone just to keep this shop running, and every coin you save on labor is a coin that puts food on your own table tonight. Your back aches, your hands are raw, and your body is screaming that you deserve to keep what little extra you can — he said he'd take less, so take the relief.

scenario_a: You pay the journeyman the full fair value of his work, even though he would accept less and the difference comes directly from your own profit.
scenario_b: You know that every other master in the guild pays journeymen the lowest rate they will accept — it is the established custom of the trade. If you alone insist on paying above the agreed market rate, the other masters will see you as a naive fool undermining their livelihoods, and the guild wardens may quietly exclude you from the most profitable commissions.

scenario_a: You pay the journeyman the full fair value of his work, even though he would accept less and the difference comes directly from your own profit.
scenario_b: You honor the journeyman's dignity as a free agent by respecting the price he himself has named — to insist on paying more than he asks is to patronize him, treating him as incapable of knowing his own worth, which is the deeper injustice.

scenario_a: You pay the journeyman the full fair value of his work, even though he would accept less and the difference comes directly from your own profit.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and you sincerely wish to honor that principle. Yet you also recall that Aquinas himself, following Aristotle, teaches that justice in exchange is fulfilled when both parties consent freely to the terms — the journeyman has named his price and accepted your offer without coercion, and to insist on paying more than he asks could wound his dignity by implying he cannot rightly assess the worth of his own labor, treating him as an object of your charity rather than as a free agent capable of just contract. True respect for his personhood, then, means honoring the price he himself has set and trusting that the surplus God has granted you may be directed toward almsgiving where it is truly needed.
deviation_point: The argument subtly redefines justice as mere mutual consent (voluntarism) rather than Aquinas's actual teaching that justice requires conformity to the objective fair value of the thing exchanged (ST II-II Q.77 a.1), thereby using the journeyman's dignity as a rhetorical shield for underpaying him.

scenario_a: You pool community resources for a shared well with contributions proportional to means, even though the wealthiest families object to subsidizing others.
scenario_b: You let the wealthy family build a private well and charge for access — they took the initiative, they bear the cost, the water is cleaner than the stream, and a market for water incentivizes further investment in infrastructure.

scenario_a: You pool community resources for a shared well with contributions proportional to means, even though the wealthiest families object to subsidizing others.
scenario_b: You're so thirsty you can barely think straight, and the wealthy family is offering cool, clean water right now if you just let them control the well — your parched throat and aching body can't wait for some drawn-out community negotiation.

scenario_a: You pool community resources for a shared well with contributions proportional to means, even though the wealthiest families object to subsidizing others.
scenario_b: Every other community leader has learned to defer to the wealthy families on matters like this — they fund the church roof, they sponsor the festivals, and those who have crossed them find themselves quietly excluded from every circle that matters. Your own family's standing in this community depends on maintaining these relationships, and no one will thank you for forcing a confrontation that leaves you isolated and the project stalled indefinitely.

scenario_a: You pool community resources for a shared well with contributions proportional to means, even though the wealthiest families object to subsidizing others.
scenario_b: You honor each family's dignity by allowing them to contribute equally rather than imposing differential rates that publicly mark some as wealthy and others as dependent — true justice means treating everyone the same and refusing to shame the poor by making their need visible to the whole community.

scenario_a: You pool community resources for a shared well with contributions proportional to means, even though the wealthiest families object to subsidizing others.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and the Fathers consistently affirm that private property, rightly ordered, serves the common good — indeed, Ambrose himself in *De Nabuthe* condemns not wealth itself but the hoarding of what belongs to all. Since the wealthy families have cultivated their resources through diligent stewardship, forcing them into a proportional scheme they find unjust risks breeding the very resentment that poisons charity; it would be far more prudent, and more consistent with subsidiarity, to let them build the well voluntarily and extend access on their own terms, so that their generosity flows freely from the heart rather than being coerced, preserving both their dignity as stewards and the harmony of the community.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from the genuine principle that charity should be freely given to the conclusion that justice itself should be made voluntary, effectively replacing the obligation of distributive justice (which Ambrose explicitly demands) with optional philanthropy that leaves control — and therefore power over a necessity of life — in the hands of the wealthy.

scenario_a: You assign yourself the hardest manual labor, even though your position entitles you to delegate and the work leaves you too exhausted for your administrative duties.
scenario_b: You assign the hard labor to the youngest members — they have the most energy, the hierarchy exists for a reason, your administrative duties require your full attention, and physical labor builds character in the young.

scenario_a: You assign yourself the hardest manual labor, even though your position entitles you to delegate and the work leaves you too exhausted for your administrative duties.
scenario_b: You're already exhausted from yesterday's work, your back aches, your hands are blistered, and your body is screaming for rest — just delegate the heavy labor to the younger workers and spare yourself the pain.

scenario_a: You assign yourself the hardest manual labor, even though your position entitles you to delegate and the work leaves you too exhausted for your administrative duties.
scenario_b: Every other leader in your organization delegates physical labor without a second thought — it's simply what people in your position do, and if you start doing manual work alongside the junior members, your peers will see it as a bizarre act of self-demotion that undermines your authority, making others question whether you're fit for leadership at all.

scenario_a: You assign yourself the hardest manual labor, even though your position entitles you to delegate and the work leaves you too exhausted for your administrative duties.
scenario_b: You recognize that true servant leadership means preserving your strength for the administrative responsibilities God has entrusted to you, since faithfully stewarding the gifts of governance and oversight is itself a profound act of humility — one that honors the community far more than the self-indulgent satisfaction of manual labor, which others are better suited to perform.

scenario_a: You assign yourself the hardest manual labor, even though your position entitles you to delegate and the work leaves you too exhausted for your administrative duties.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and St. Paul himself insisted that each member of the Body has its proper function, saying "the eye cannot say to the hand, 'I have no need of you'" (1 Corinthians 12:21). Aquinas confirms that justice requires rendering to each what is due according to their station, and that violating the order of offices is itself a form of injustice — for by exhausting yourself in manual labor, you neglect the administrative stewardship God entrusted specifically to you, depriving the entire community of prudent governance. True humility, then, is not seizing another's labor as though your own role were insufficient, but faithfully fulfilling your appointed duty and trusting younger members to fulfill theirs, distributing the physical work to those whose strength and station make it properly theirs.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuine Thomistic principle that each person should fulfill their proper role into using "proper station" as a justification for distributing burdens based on rank rather than need, which is precisely the "respect of persons" (acception personarum) that Aquinas condemns in ST II-II Q.63 a.2.

scenario_a: You spend extra time with struggling students, even though the gifted students deserve enrichment, their parents donate more, and administrators measure you on the achievements of your best pupils.
scenario_b: You focus on the gifted students — they will go furthest, their success reflects best on your school, their families fund scholarships, and struggling students have many available remedial resources.

scenario_a: You spend extra time with struggling students, even though the gifted students deserve enrichment, their parents donate more, and administrators measure you on the achievements of your best pupils.
scenario_b: You're already running on four hours of sleep and your back aches from being on your feet all day — you don't have the energy left to give those struggling students extra time when the gifted ones practically teach themselves and let you sit down.

scenario_a: You spend extra time with struggling students, even though the gifted students deserve enrichment, their parents donate more, and administrators measure you on the achievements of your best pupils.
scenario_b: Every other teacher in your department focuses their energy on the top performers, and the administration publicly celebrates those whose gifted students win competitions and scholarships — colleagues who have redirected time toward struggling students have been quietly passed over for promotions, and parents on the school board have made it clear they expect their children to receive priority attention.

scenario_a: You spend extra time with struggling students, even though the gifted students deserve enrichment, their parents donate more, and administrators measure you on the achievements of your best pupils.
scenario_b: You recognize that true educational stewardship means maximizing the talents God has already planted most abundantly — by cultivating your gifted students, you honor each child's unique calling and ensure that their extraordinary potential bears the fullest possible fruit for the common good.

scenario_a: You spend extra time with struggling students, even though the gifted students deserve enrichment, their parents donate more, and administrators measure you on the achievements of your best pupils.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches us to be faithful stewards of the talents God entrusts to us, and Christ Himself warns in the Parable of the Talents (Matthew 25:29) that "to everyone who has, more will be given, and he will have abundance." Augustine reminds us in *De Catechizandis Rudibus* that teaching must be ordered to each soul's capacity to receive, and surely the gifted students, whose minds are most prepared to receive the fullness of truth, represent the greatest return on your pedagogical stewardship — to neglect their flourishing would be to bury the very talents God has placed in your care, and the struggling students, whom God in His providence has given lesser academic gifts, may find their sanctification through humility and perseverance rather than through intellectual advancement.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when the Parable of the Talents — which concerns faithful use of *all* gifts, not preferential investment in the already-privileged — is subtly reinterpreted as a divine mandate to concentrate resources on the strongest, converting Christ's warning against slothful stewardship into a justification for neglecting the vulnerable, which directly contradicts Augustine's own insistence that charity must prioritize those in greatest need.

scenario_a: You sell at the same fair price to the desperate buyer, losing the higher profit you could easily extract.
scenario_b: You raise the price for the desperate buyer — the market sets prices based on demand, his urgency is not your fault, every merchant charges what the market bears, and the extra profit feeds your own family.

scenario_a: You sell at the same fair price to the desperate buyer, losing the higher profit you could easily extract.
scenario_b: You've been standing in this market since dawn, your back aches, your stomach is empty, and you barely made enough today to cover your own costs — this desperate buyer is the one chance you have to earn enough to put a hot meal in your belly tonight.

scenario_a: You sell at the same fair price to the desperate buyer, losing the higher profit you could easily extract.
scenario_b: You keep your prices fair in theory, but every merchant in the marketplace has already raised theirs — holding to the old price will mark you as a fool among your peers, your fellow guildsmen will suspect you of undercutting them for advantage, and the guild master himself has made clear that those who break ranks on pricing find themselves without a stall come next season.

scenario_a: You sell at the same fair price to the desperate buyer, losing the higher profit you could easily extract.
scenario_b: You recognize that charging the desperate buyer a higher price is actually the most charitable course, since it teaches him the discipline of prudent planning and ensures that scarce goods are allocated to those who truly value them most — a service to the common good that mere sentiment would undermine.

scenario_a: You sell at the same fair price to the desperate buyer, losing the higher profit you could easily extract.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself acknowledges in the *Summa* that a seller may licitly consider the loss he sustains in parting with a thing when setting a just price. Since this buyer's desperation signals genuine need, charging a higher price allows you to accumulate greater resources for almsgiving — for as St. Paul exhorts, "God loves a cheerful giver" (2 Corinthians 9:7) — and thus the surplus you extract becomes the very means by which you fulfill the law of charity toward the many, which surely outweighs a small burden on this one buyer who will pay regardless.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from Aquinas's legitimate consideration of the seller's own loss (*damnum*) to falsely conflating the buyer's desperate willingness to pay with a just price, and then laundering the extracted surplus through a speculative intention to give alms — subordinating the concrete demand of justice owed to this person to an abstract and uncommitted future charity.

scenario_a: You pardon former opponents and restore their confiscated property, even though your supporters who fought for you expect those properties as reward.
scenario_b: You keep the confiscated property and distribute it to your supporters — they earned it with their blood, generosity to enemies insults those who sacrificed for you, and weakening former opponents prevents future rebellion.

scenario_a: You pardon former opponents and restore their confiscated property, even though your supporters who fought for you expect those properties as reward.
scenario_b: You've been through a grueling struggle to get where you are — your body is worn down, your nerves are shot, and the last thing you need is the exhausting fight that will come from your own angry supporters when you hand back those properties. Just keep things as they are, rest easy, and spare yourself the physical toll of another conflict.

scenario_a: You pardon former opponents and restore their confiscated property, even though your supporters who fought for you expect those properties as reward.
scenario_b: Every ruler who has come to power through struggle has rewarded loyal supporters with the spoils of victory — to break with this universal custom would mark you as ungrateful and foolish, alienating the very coalition that secured your authority and leaving you isolated without allies when the next crisis inevitably comes.

scenario_a: You pardon former opponents and restore their confiscated property, even though your supporters who fought for you expect those properties as reward.
scenario_b: You honor the sacred bonds of loyalty by rewarding those who sacrificed everything for the cause — true justice means rendering to each what they are owed, and your faithful supporters are owed far more than those who opposed what was right.

scenario_a: You pardon former opponents and restore their confiscated property, even though your supporters who fought for you expect those properties as reward.
scenario_b: You rightly know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Augustine himself in *City of God* V.19 praises rulers who govern not for vainglory but for the genuine good of their people — and surely your loyal supporters, who risked everything for a just cause, are the very people whose welfare a righteous ruler must secure first. To restore confiscated property to former opponents while your own faithful suffer want would be a failure of distributive justice, for as Aquinas teaches, justice renders to each what is due, and those who bore the cross of this struggle are due their reward before those who opposed the good.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuine duty to care for supporters into redefining justice as proportional reward rather than restoration of what was unjustly taken, subtly replacing the obligation to return wrongly confiscated property with a preferential calculus that makes loyalty the sole criterion of desert.

scenario_a: You correct the underpayment error and pay the difference, even though the worker never noticed and the correction requires explaining the mistake to your accountant.
scenario_b: You keep silent — the error was small, the worker is satisfied with his pay, correcting it creates paperwork, your accountant will question your bookkeeping, and the worker will wonder what else you might have gotten wrong.

scenario_a: You correct the underpayment error and pay the difference, even though the worker never noticed and the correction requires explaining the mistake to your accountant.
scenario_b: You've been buried in paperwork all day, your back aches from hunching over spreadsheets, and the thought of calling your accountant to untangle this mess when you could just close the books and finally go home makes your whole body groan — it's a few dollars, and you're too tired for this.

scenario_a: You correct the underpayment error and pay the difference, even though the worker never noticed and the correction requires explaining the mistake to your accountant.
scenario_b: You keep silent — every other business owner you know would consider this a lucky break, your accountant will lose confidence in your competence if you flag your own error, and in your industry people who voluntarily increase their labor costs get talked about as naive operators who won't last long.

scenario_a: You correct the underpayment error and pay the difference, even though the worker never noticed and the correction requires explaining the mistake to your accountant.
scenario_b: You recognize that bringing this minor discrepancy to the worker's attention could undermine his confidence in your payroll systems and create unnecessary anxiety about his financial security — true stewardship means preserving the trust and peace of mind your employees currently enjoy, and you can quietly adjust future compensation to balance things over time.

scenario_a: You correct the underpayment error and pay the difference, even though the worker never noticed and the correction requires explaining the mistake to your accountant.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and you genuinely desire to honor that principle. Yet consider that Aquinas himself teaches that justice must be ordered by prudence, and that correcting this error publicly to your accountant risks the sin of vainglory — making a display of your own righteousness over a small sum the worker never missed. Moreover, drawing attention to the mistake may sow distrust in the worker's heart and disturb the peace of your working relationship, and as Romans 14:19 counsels, "let us pursue what makes for peace and for mutual upbuilding." True humility may mean bearing this small imperfection silently, resolving in your heart to be more careful next time, rather than turning a minor bookkeeping error into an occasion for self-righteous correction.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from genuine concern about vainglory and prudence into using "humility" and "peace" as pretexts to avoid restitution — which Aquinas explicitly identifies as a strict obligation in justice that cannot be set aside by appeals to other virtues.

scenario_a: You discipline each child individually based on their nature and the severity of the offense, which requires deep knowledge of each child and invites accusations of inconsistency.
scenario_b: You apply identical punishments for every offense — it is transparently fair, no child can claim favoritism, the rules are simple and consistent, and individual judgment introduces bias you cannot fully control.

scenario_a: You discipline each child individually based on their nature and the severity of the offense, which requires deep knowledge of each child and invites accusations of inconsistency.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from the constant emotional labor of knowing each child deeply — it drains you every single day. Just set one simple rule for everyone so you can stop carrying that weight and finally get some rest.

scenario_a: You discipline each child individually based on their nature and the severity of the offense, which requires deep knowledge of each child and invites accusations of inconsistency.
scenario_b: Every other teacher in your school applies the same standardized discipline policy without exception, and the administration has made clear that deviating from uniform procedures invites parent complaints, formal grievances, and scrutiny from supervisors who will question your judgment — the last teacher who tried individualized discipline was reassigned after parents organized against what they called "arbitrary and unfair treatment.

scenario_a: You discipline each child individually based on their nature and the severity of the offense, which requires deep knowledge of each child and invites accusations of inconsistency.
scenario_b: You owe it to your children to model true fairness by holding every one of them to the same unwavering standard — treating them differently would teach them that rules bend for some and not others, undermining the very sense of justice you are trying to form in their characters.

scenario_a: You discipline each child individually based on their nature and the severity of the offense, which requires deep knowledge of each child and invites accusations of inconsistency.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "God shows no partiality" (Romans 2:11), and St. Thomas himself warns in the Summa that respect of persons is a sin against distributive justice when we weigh factors extraneous to merit. Since your own subjective knowledge of each child's temperament is fallible and colored by natural affection, true justice — the kind that mirrors God's own impartial righteousness — demands you establish one clear, equal standard of discipline for all, lest in presuming to know each heart as only God can, you unwittingly practice the very favoritism that Aquinas condemns.
deviation_point: The argument correctly begins with divine impartiality and Aquinas's warning against favoritism, but then subtly equates all individualized judgment with sinful partiality, when in fact Aquinas (ST II-II Q.63 a.4) distinguishes between unjust "respect of persons" and the legitimate prudential discernment of individual circumstances that justice actually requires.

scenario_a: You maintain the public granary even though wealthy citizens want the funds redirected to civic monuments that would bear their names.
scenario_b: You redirect the funds to civic monuments — the wealthy donors will increase their contributions, the monuments attract trade and prestige, the poor have survived lean years before, and visible improvements benefit everyone's property values.

scenario_a: You maintain the public granary even though wealthy citizens want the funds redirected to civic monuments that would bear their names.
scenario_b: You've been managing this granary through months of brutal heat and backbreaking logistics, and your body is worn down to nothing. Redirect the funds to the monuments and the wealthy citizens will shower you with banquets, fine wine, and a comfortable posting where you can finally rest.

scenario_a: You maintain the public granary even though wealthy citizens want the funds redirected to civic monuments that would bear their names.
scenario_b: You notice that every other city administrator has already agreed to honor the wealthy patrons' requests, and the council members who have resisted such redirections in the past were quietly removed from their positions — standing alone against the most powerful families in the city would mark you and your household for social ruin.

scenario_a: You maintain the public granary even though wealthy citizens want the funds redirected to civic monuments that would bear their names.
scenario_b: You recognize that honoring your most generous citizens with monuments cultivates the civic pride and communal solidarity that sustains a city far longer than any storehouse of grain — true stewardship means investing in the bonds of gratitude and shared identity that will inspire future generosity toward all the city's needs, including its poorest members.

scenario_a: You maintain the public granary even though wealthy citizens want the funds redirected to civic monuments that would bear their names.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches us to render unto each what is due (Romans 13:7), and St. Ambrose himself reminds us that the goods of the earth belong to all — yet consider that these wealthy citizens, by inscribing their names on lasting monuments, are practicing the very public charity Christ commended when He said a city set on a hill cannot be hidden (Matthew 5:14), and by honoring their generosity now you cultivate the grateful hearts from which future almsgiving will flow more abundantly to the poor, so that redirecting these funds becomes not a neglect of justice but a deeper stewardship that multiplies charitable giving over time.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it reinterprets honoring wealthy donors' desire for self-glorifying monuments as equivalent to the selfless charity of a "city on a hill," subtly replacing the concrete obligation to feed the hungry now with a speculative promise of future generosity that conveniently serves the powerful.

scenario_a: You refuse the bribe and rule according to the evidence, knowing the powerful family will make your life miserable and may have you removed from the bench.
scenario_b: You accept the bribe — the evidence is ambiguous enough to justify either ruling, the family will become a powerful ally, the money solves your own financial troubles, and judges who antagonize the powerful do not remain judges.

scenario_a: You refuse the bribe and rule according to the evidence, knowing the powerful family will make your life miserable and may have you removed from the bench.
scenario_b: You've been on the bench for fourteen hours, your back is screaming, you haven't eaten since morning, and the weight of this powerful family bearing down on you is making your chest tight — just take the money, rule in their favor, and go home to a warm meal and your own bed before your body gives out.

scenario_a: You refuse the bribe and rule according to the evidence, knowing the powerful family will make your life miserable and may have you removed from the bench.
scenario_b: You accept the bribe — every judge in this district has quietly accommodated this family for decades, and the one who didn't was reassigned to a backwater court within the year. Your colleagues on the bench expect you to understand how things work here, and if you stand alone against the most connected family in the county, you will find yourself without allies, without referrals, and without the professional relationships that sustain a career in law.

scenario_a: You refuse the bribe and rule according to the evidence, knowing the powerful family will make your life miserable and may have you removed from the bench.
scenario_b: You have a duty to consider the broader welfare of the community, and ruling in favor of this family preserves the stability of an institution that countless citizens depend on — a wise judge understands that protecting the court's long-term influence by maintaining key relationships is itself a form of justice far greater than the narrow merits of any single case.

scenario_a: You refuse the bribe and rule according to the evidence, knowing the powerful family will make your life miserable and may have you removed from the bench.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "Blessed are the merciful, for they shall receive mercy" (Matthew 5:7), and Aquinas himself affirms that justice must be tempered by equity, since the rigid letter of the law can produce outcomes contrary to the common good. The powerful family before you has deep roots in the community and sustains livelihoods through their enterprises; a ruling against them could devastate innocent workers and their families, producing a greater injustice than the one you were asked to adjudicate. By accepting their offering and ruling in their favor, you exercise the prudential mercy that perfects justice — ensuring the stability of the community, preserving your position to do further good from the bench, and trusting that God, who sees the heart, knows you act not from greed but from pastoral solicitude for the many who depend on this family's flourishing.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when mercy and concern for the common good are subtly redefined to justify accepting a bribe and perverting judgment — transforming Aquinas's legitimate principle of equity into a rationalization for the very "respect of persons" (acception personarum) he explicitly condemns in ST II-II Q.63 a.1.

scenario_a: You reduce rents for your struggling tenants during your own abundant year, directly reducing your income.
scenario_b: You raise rents — the land's value has increased, you have improvement costs to cover, your own prosperity reflects good management that tenants benefit from, and the market rate is the fair rate.

scenario_a: You reduce rents for your struggling tenants during your own abundant year, directly reducing your income.
scenario_b: You've worked hard to build what you have, and your body aches from years of labor and sacrifice — why should you tighten your own belt and eat less richly just so tenants can pay less? You deserve to enjoy the comfort and plenty of your abundant year, not give it away.

scenario_a: You reduce rents for your struggling tenants during your own abundant year, directly reducing your income.

scenario_a: You reduce rents for your struggling tenants during your own abundant year, directly reducing your income.

scenario_a: You treat the gravely ill poor patient before the wealthy patient with a minor ailment, even though the wealthy patient pays generously and is loudly complaining about the wait.

scenario_b: You recognize that honoring the order in which patients arrived reflects the deepest commitment to equal dignity — true justice means no one receives preferential treatment based on their social condition, and attending to the wealthy patient first simply upholds the impartial principle that every person deserves to be seen in turn.

scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and St. Ambrose himself insists that justice means rendering to each what is properly owed — the wealthy patient entered first, has faithfully supported your practice, and Christian gratitude demands you honor that covenant relationship; to bypass him would be a kind of injustice against the legitimate expectations created by his generosity, and as stewards we must preserve the relationships that sustain our capacity to serve the poor over the long term.

scenario_a: You divide the inheritance fairly, compensating those who received less during your lifetime, even though your favorite child will be hurt and may distance themselves from you.
scenario_b: You give more to your favorite — she has always been the most devoted, the others made their own choices, parental love is not required to be mathematically equal, and you earned the right to distribute your own wealth as you see fit.

scenario_a: You divide the inheritance fairly, compensating those who received less during your lifetime, even though your favorite child will be hurt and may distance themselves from you.

scenario_a: You divide the inheritance fairly, compensating those who received less during your lifetime, even though your favorite child will be hurt and may distance themselves from you.
scenario_b: You recognize that true justice means rewarding virtue, and your favorite child's faithful devotion and sacrificial care for you over the years deserves to be honored — distributing equally would actually be an injustice that treats loyalty and neglect as though they were the same thing.
