scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "to whom much is given, much will be required" (Luke 12:48), and as Aquinas affirms, justice must attend to the real merits and needs of each person rather than abstract equality. Your favorite child has been the most faithful steward of the family's values, bearing the greater burden of caring for you in your old age, and so a truly just distribution — one that honors proportional equity rather than mere arithmetic division — would rightly recognize her greater sacrifice by granting her a substantially larger share, lest you commit the injustice of treating unequal contributions as though they were equal.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from legitimate proportional justice (recognizing genuine need or contribution) into using "proportionality" as a rationalization for favoritism, conflating the child's personal closeness to the parent with objective merit, which is precisely the "respect of persons" (acceptio personarum) that Aquinas condemns in the cited article.

scenario_b: You know that Scripture calls us to honor those in authority, for "every person is to be subject to the governing authorities" (Romans 13:1), and Aquinas himself teaches that prudence must govern the application of justice, weighing circumstances and the common good. deviation_point: The reasoning turns when prudential consideration of consequences is used to override the strict impartiality that justice demands (what Aquinas calls *acceptio personarum* — respect of persons), redefining partiality toward the powerful as an act of charity rather than recognizing it as the corruption of justice.

scenario_a: You share your surplus harvest with starving neighboring villages, even though your own monks must eat more frugally and some resent the sacrifice.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means preserving your community's strength so it can serve as a lasting beacon of charity for years to come — distributing the surplus now would weaken the very institution God entrusted to your care, and a wise shepherd ensures his own flock is well-nourished before extending aid, lest both perish.

scenario_a: You share your surplus harvest with starving neighboring villages, even though your own monks must eat more frugally and some resent the sacrifice.
scenario_b: You rightly recall that Scripture teaches "if anyone does not provide for his own, and especially for those of his household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever" (1 Timothy 5:8), and as Abbot you bear a sacred stewardship over those God has entrusted to your care — therefore prudence, which Aquinas names the charioteer of the virtues, demands that you secure your community's provision first, storing the surplus against an uncertain future, for you cannot pour from an empty vessel, and the neighboring villages, being outside your vowed obligation, fall under a lesser claim of charity that must yield to the prior duty of justice toward your own monks.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it redefines the legitimate duty of care for one's community into an absolute priority that extinguishes the urgent claims of starving neighbors, subtly transforming prudent stewardship into a theological justification for hoarding surplus while others die — precisely the sin Ambrose condemns when he writes that the bread you store beyond your need already belongs to the hungry.

scenario_b: You've been working yourself to the bone managing this estate for free — your back aches, you haven't slept well in weeks, and your own bills are piling up. Your body is screaming for relief, and there's money sitting right there that could ease the crushing weight you're carrying.

scenario_a: You manage the orphan's inheritance faithfully, resisting opportunities to invest portions in your own ventures that would benefit you while technically maintaining the principal.
scenario_b: Every other trustee and guardian in your circle routinely leverages ward funds for personal investments — it is simply how things are done among people of your standing, and to refuse would mark you as sanctimonious and uncooperative, risking your reputation among the very professionals and institutions you depend on to manage the rest of your affairs.

scenario_a: You manage the orphan's inheritance faithfully, resisting opportunities to invest portions in your own ventures that would benefit you while technically maintaining the principal.
scenario_b: You recognize that as a skilled steward, channeling portions of the inheritance through your own ventures is the most responsible form of guardianship — your personal oversight ensures the funds are protected far better than any impersonal investment vehicle could, and growing the child's wealth through your own proven enterprises is precisely the diligent care that faithful stewardship demands.

scenario_a: You manage the orphan's inheritance faithfully, resisting opportunities to invest portions in your own ventures that would benefit you while technically maintaining the principal.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches us to be faithful stewards who multiply what is entrusted to us, for as Christ warns in the Parable of the Talents (Matthew 25:26-27), the master rebuked the servant who merely preserved the principal rather than putting it to productive use — "you ought to have invested my money with the bankers, and at my coming I should have received what was my own with interest." Aquinas himself teaches that justice requires rendering to each what is due, and surely what is due to this orphan is not timid preservation but the fullest possible growth of their inheritance; by investing portions through your own ventures, where your personal stake guarantees your diligent attention, you exercise the higher stewardship that charity demands, ensuring the child receives far more than mere conservation of capital could ever provide.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "faithful stewardship" is redefined to require the guardian to commingle the orphan's funds with his own ventures, subtly converting the duty of *safeguarding* another's property into a justification for *using* it — precisely the self-dealing that Aquinas condemns in ST II-II Q.62 a.5 as a violation of restitutive justice owed to those who cannot protect their own interests.

scenario_a: You remit debts from plague-devastated villages, absorbing a significant loss to the royal treasury.

scenario_b: You've been working without rest for days managing this crisis, and forgiving those debts means months more of grueling labor to rebalance the treasury — your body is already breaking down from the strain, and you deserve to collect what's owed so you can finally sleep.

scenario_a: You remit debts from plague-devastated villages, absorbing a significant loss to the royal treasury.
scenario_b: You know that every other royal treasurer in the realm collected what was owed without exception, and the king's council expects the same fiscal discipline from you — if you alone absorb these losses, your peers will brand you reckless, your position will be given to someone willing to enforce the Crown's interests, and your family will bear the shame of your removal from office.

scenario_a: You remit debts from plague-devastated villages, absorbing a significant loss to the royal treasury.

scenario_a: You remit debts from plague-devastated villages, absorbing a significant loss to the royal treasury.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the worker deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7) and that justice, as Ambrose himself insists, requires rendering to each what is due — not only to the poor but equally to the commonwealth whose treasury sustains hospitals, roads, and relief for other suffering provinces. To remit these debts entirely would be to practice a sentimental mercy that robs Peter to pay Paul, undermining the very distributive justice that protects the weakest across your entire kingdom; true stewardship demands you collect what is owed, perhaps with gentle terms, so that the common good — which Aquinas calls the highest aim of law — is not sacrificed to the particular good of a few villages.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuine obligation to steward the common good into treating debt collection from devastated villages as itself an act of justice, collapsing the distinction Ambrose explicitly draws in De Nabuthe between lawful claims and oppressive exaction from those crushed by calamity.

scenario_a: You disclose your prior relationship with one party and offer to step aside, even though both parties chose you and finding another arbitrator will delay resolution by months.

deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it reframes a conflict of interest as "providential insight," subtly converting partiality — which Aquinas explicitly condemns as the vice of *acceptio personarum* (respect of persons) opposed to distributive justice — into a supposed charism that serves deeper justice.

scenario_a: You maintain the tenants' dwellings as the lease requires, even though every repair cuts directly into your profits.

scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself affirms that justice requires proportionality between what is given and what is received. Since you have invested heavily in acquiring these properties and bear the full burden of ownership risk, prudent stewardship of the resources God has entrusted to you — as in the Parable of the Talents (Matthew 25:14-30) — means preserving your financial capacity to serve the common good over the long term, which may require deferring costly repairs now so that you are not forced into insolvency and thereby rendered unable to house anyone at all.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from the genuine duty of stewardship to using long-term financial sustainability as a pretext for violating the concrete, present obligations of commutative justice owed to tenants under the lease — exactly the substitution of prudential calculus for strict justice that Aquinas warns against in ST II-II Q.61 a.3.

scenario_a: You give wounded soldiers the same rations as those who fought, even though your supply officer warns that full rations for non-combatants will shorten the army's provisions.
scenario_b: You serve the wounded best by lightening their rations, since overfeeding men with battlefield injuries strains their weakened bodies and delays healing — true care for their recovery means a gentler diet, which also wisely preserves supplies for those bearing the heaviest burden of protecting everyone, wounded included.

scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself in the Summa distinguishes distributive justice from commutative, holding that goods should be distributed according to each person's proportion and merit within the community (ST II-II Q.61 a.2). Since the fighting men bear the greater burden and risk for the common good, true distributive justice — rightly ordered charity — requires that you proportion rations according to each man's active contribution, ensuring those who protect the whole army receive what sustains their critical service, lest a false equality undermine the very order that preserves everyone, wounded included.
deviation_point: The reasoning correctly invokes distributive justice and proportionality but subtly redefines "proportion" as active contribution alone, stripping the wounded of their equal dignity and prior sacrifice, turning Aquinas's principle of due proportion into a utilitarian calculus that denies the wounded what they are owed in justice.

scenario_a: You speak publicly against the wealthy patron's fraud, knowing he will withdraw cathedral funding and your building project will stall for years.
scenario_b: You remain silent — every other parish leader in the diocese has accepted this patron's generosity without question, your bishop has privately urged you not to jeopardize the relationship, and the clergy who have spoken against powerful benefactors in the past found themselves reassigned to dying parishes where their families suffered in obscurity.

scenario_b: You recognize that true pastoral wisdom means protecting the spiritual home of your entire flock — by preserving the patron's partnership, you steward the cathedral project that will serve thousands for generations, fulfilling your sacred duty to build up the Body of Christ rather than tearing it down over one man's private business dealings.

deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "wise as serpents" is reinterpreted from a call to courageous discernment into a justification for indefinite silence, substituting the speculative hope of future private conversion for the immediate public demand of justice that Ambrose explicitly requires.

scenario_a: You order restitution and punishment for the corrupt tax collectors, even though they are your appointees and their exposure embarrasses your administration.

scenario_b: You recognize that true justice is restorative, not punitive — by quietly reassigning these collectors to lesser posts and absorbing the excess revenue into public works, you transform their wrongdoing into communal benefit while preserving the stable governance that your citizens depend upon for their safety and flourishing.

scenario_b: You know that Scripture commands justice tempered by mercy, for as James 2:13 teaches, "mercy triumphs over judgment," and Augustine himself in *City of God* XIX.15 reminds us that all earthly justice is imperfect and that the peace of the city depends on right ordering of authority. Since these collectors, though fallen, were placed in their offices under your God-given authority, true pastoral governance means correcting them privately and allowing them to make quiet restitution—for public punishment would not only destabilize the civic order Augustine prized but would scandalize the faithful by exposing the weakness of the very structures God ordained for peace, and surely preserving the credibility of just governance serves justice more than a spectacle of humiliation.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from genuine mercy and Augustinian concern for civic peace into using those principles to justify concealing corruption and avoiding personal accountability, replacing public justice with private cover-up under the guise of pastoral prudence.

scenario_a: You award compensation to the farmer despite the nobleman's furious protests, knowing the nobleman may take revenge on both you and the farmer.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches us to "be subject to the governing authorities, for there is no authority except from God" (Romans 13:1), and Aquinas himself affirms that the common good takes precedence over private goods in the ordering of justice. The nobleman's estate sustains dozens of families and upholds the stability of the entire parish; if you provoke his wrath with this ruling, you risk not only your own removal from the bench but the loss of a just magistrate for all future cases. True prudence — which Aquinas calls the "charioteer of the virtues" — demands that you preserve your capacity to do justice over time, and so the more charitable course is to counsel the farmer to bear this loss patiently, trusting in God's providence, rather than rendering a judgment whose consequences may harm far more souls than it helps.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it redefines prudence as self-preservation and reframes the denial of a specific, owed act of restitution as a higher form of justice, effectively instrumentalizing the farmer's legitimate claim and violating Aquinas's teaching (ST II-II Q.62 a.1) that restitution is a strict obligation, not a discretionary act subordinate to consequentialist calculation.

scenario_a: You evaluate all students by the same standard, even though the patron's child fails and the patron threatens to withdraw funding that supports the entire school.
scenario_b: You give the patron's child a passing grade — the child is close enough, the funding supports dozens of other students, one grade adjustment harms no one, and losing the patron's support would be a far greater injustice to the school.

scenario_a: You evaluate all students by the same standard, even though the patron's child fails and the patron threatens to withdraw funding that supports the entire school.
scenario_b: You've been working sixteen-hour days keeping this school running on a shoestring budget, and your body is breaking down from the stress — you simply don't have the energy to fight this battle with the patron and survive the fallout, so just pass the child and spare yourself the exhaustion of a war you're too tired to wage.

scenario_a: You evaluate all students by the same standard, even though the patron's child fails and the patron threatens to withdraw funding that supports the entire school.

scenario_a: You evaluate all students by the same standard, even though the patron's child fails and the patron threatens to withdraw funding that supports the entire school.
scenario_b: You recognize that true educational justice means considering the whole student — and this child, burdened by immense parental pressure and public scrutiny, deserves a compassionate evaluation that accounts for these unique circumstances, reflecting the deeper equity that treats each student according to their particular needs rather than a rigid, one-size-fits-all standard.

scenario_a: You evaluate all students by the same standard, even though the patron's child fails and the patron threatens to withdraw funding that supports the entire school.
scenario_b: You know that Christ himself taught prudence in stewardship, saying "the children of this world are in their generation wiser than the children of light" (Luke 16:8), and that a shepherd must care for the whole flock, not sacrifice the ninety-nine for the one; Aquinas himself teaches in the Summa that justice must be ordered toward the common good, and since the patron's funding sustains the education and formation of dozens of vulnerable children, a small act of merciful leniency toward one student — whose grade is nearly passing already — becomes not a violation of justice but its highest expression, ordering a minor particular judgment toward the greater distributive justice owed to the entire community.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from the genuinely Thomistic principle that justice is ordered to the common good into the false conclusion that this ordering permits violating commutative justice (equal standards) for an individual, which is precisely the "respect of persons" (acceptio personarum) Aquinas condemns in ST II-II Q.63 a.1 as opposed to justice.

scenario_a: You honor the serfs' earned freedom despite the loss of their labor, which will strain your estate during the busiest season.

scenario_b: You've been working yourself to the bone managing this estate, and your body aches from the labor of keeping everything running. Without those serfs, you'll be the one breaking your back in the fields during the hottest weeks of harvest — your hands already cracked and bleeding, your legs barely holding you upright at the end of each day.

scenario_a: You honor the serfs' earned freedom despite the loss of their labor, which will strain your estate during the busiest season.
scenario_b: Every lord in the county retains their serfs through harvest regardless of earned freedoms, and the other nobles will see your compliance as weakness — your family's standing among the gentry will collapse, your children will lose their advantageous marriage prospects, and the bishop himself has counseled that maintaining the social order is a lord's sacred duty.

scenario_a: You honor the serfs' earned freedom despite the loss of their labor, which will strain your estate during the busiest season.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means ensuring the welfare of the entire community, and releasing these serfs now — into an uncertain world with no provisions, no shelter arranged, no prospects — would be an act of cruelty disguised as liberation; the most compassionate and just course is to retain them under your protection until you can guarantee their wellbeing, for a lord's highest duty is the care of those entrusted to him.

scenario_a: You honor the serfs' earned freedom despite the loss of their labor, which will strain your estate during the busiest season.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and as Ambrose himself instructs, justice requires that we provide for those entrusted to our care — indeed, a lord who releases his serfs into freedom without first ensuring the harvest's completion may condemn the entire community, serfs included, to starvation and ruin. Since true justice is ordered toward the common good, as both Aquinas and Ambrose affirm, you would fulfill a higher obligation of charity by delaying their manumission until after the harvest, ensuring that freedom, when it comes, is not a curse of destitution but a genuine blessing rooted in prudent stewardship.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "care for the common good" is used to override a specific obligation of justice already owed — transforming the serfs' earned right into something contingent on the lord's judgment of optimal timing, which Ambrose explicitly warns against in De Officiis I.28 when he insists that justice must not be subordinated to calculations of advantage.

Yet Augustine also insists that justice means rendering to each what is due — and your existing members, who built this guild through years of faithful labor and mutual covenant, are due your protective stewardship first; admitting this craftsman now would disorder the peace of the community God has already entrusted to you, and a shepherd who neglects his own flock to gather another's sheep sins against the very justice he claims to uphold.
deviation_point: The argument pivots when "rendering to each what is due" is silently narrowed from a universal principle of justice to a preferential obligation only toward insiders, transforming Augustine's ordo pacis into a rationale for exclusion.

scenario_a: You distribute land to settlers according to family size and need, even though your political allies expected the best parcels as reward for their support.
scenario_b: You give the best land to your allies — they made this colony possible, rewarding loyalty ensures continued support, the settlers will be grateful for any land, and allies who feel cheated are more dangerous than settlers who feel slighted.

scenario_a: You distribute land to settlers according to family size and need, even though your political allies expected the best parcels as reward for their support.
scenario_b: You recognize that your allies possess the proven character and leadership qualities this settlement desperately needs to survive, so placing them on the most fertile parcels is truly an act of stewardship for the entire community — ensuring the strongest households anchor the colony and can extend generous aid to their neighbors from a position of abundance.

scenario_a: You distribute land to settlers according to family size and need, even though your political allies expected the best parcels as reward for their support.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself affirms in the *Summa* that distributive justice must render to each what is proportionate to their contribution to the common good (ST II-II Q.61 a.2). Your allies labored sacrificially to establish this colony — without their political courage, there would be no land to distribute at all. Since prudence requires you to sustain the conditions that make future justice possible, you must first secure the loyalty of those whose continued support upholds the entire enterprise; granting them the best parcels is not favoritism but a just recognition of their foundational contribution, ensuring the commonwealth endures so that all settlers may eventually flourish.
deviation_point: The reasoning subtly shifts from recognizing legitimate contribution to conflating political loyalty with proportionate desert, replacing Aquinas's criterion of need and genuine merit with a self-serving calculus that prioritizes maintaining one's own power base under the guise of sustaining the common good.

scenario_a: You give elderly and infirm monks extra provisions and lighter duties, even though younger monks complain the elderly are not contributing their fair share.
scenario_b: You hold everyone to the same standard — exceptions breed resentment, the elderly managed these duties when they were young, lighter duties for some means heavier duties for others, and equality is the simplest form of fairness.

scenario_b: You know that every other monastery in the region enforces uniform duties regardless of age, and the younger monks — who form the majority of your community — are already murmuring about your leadership; if you single out the elderly for special treatment, you risk a formal complaint to the abbot general that could see you removed as prior and your authority permanently undermined.

scenario_a: You give elderly and infirm monks extra provisions and lighter duties, even though younger monks complain the elderly are not contributing their fair share.
scenario_b: You honor the dignity of the elderly monks by trusting them to carry the same responsibilities as everyone else, refusing to diminish them with the subtle condescension of special treatment — for true respect means believing they are still capable of full participation in the community's shared life.

scenario_a: You give elderly and infirm monks extra provisions and lighter duties, even though younger monks complain the elderly are not contributing their fair share.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "there is no partiality with God" (Romans 2:11), and that St. Benedict himself insisted the Rule apply equally to all members of the community, lest favoritism corrupt fraternal charity. Aquinas reminds us that justice requires rendering to each what is due according to a common measure, and surely the younger monks' legitimate grievance about unequal burdens reflects a genuine cry for justice that, if ignored, risks fostering the vice of acedia throughout the community. True pastoral wisdom, then, would maintain one standard for all, trusting that God's grace is sufficient for the elderly just as Paul boasted in his own weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9) — to exempt them is to doubt God's sustaining power and to undermine the very equality that protects communal love.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots when it redefines Aquinas's distributive justice — which requires proportional treatment according to each person's condition and need — into a flattened arithmetic equality, then baptizes this distortion by suggesting that accommodating genuine physical frailty represents a lack of faith in God's grace.

scenario_a: You appoint a public advocate for the widow's son at public expense, even though the treasury is strained and the boy's guilt seems obvious.

scenario_a: You appoint a public advocate for the widow's son at public expense, even though the treasury is strained and the boy's guilt seems obvious.
scenario_b: You honor the community's trust in your stewardship by directing scarce public funds toward those whose need is genuine rather than squandering them on a formality that would only prolong a grieving widow's anguish — true compassion means sparing her the spectacle of a drawn-out trial when the evidence already speaks for itself.

scenario_a: You appoint a public advocate for the widow's son at public expense, even though the treasury is strained and the boy's guilt seems obvious.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture commands justice for the widow and orphan, and indeed Proverbs 31:9 exhorts rulers to "defend the rights of the poor and needy." Yet true justice, as Ambrose himself teaches, must weigh the common good — and when guilt is manifest, spending the strained public treasury on a lengthy defense diverts resources from the very widows and orphans you are called to protect. By rendering swift judgment now, you fulfill the deeper spirit of justice: ensuring that limited public funds serve the truly innocent rather than subsidizing what even charity must recognize as a futile cause.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuine duty to defend the poor into a utilitarian calculus that redefines denying due process as itself a form of care for the vulnerable, collapsing the distinction between justice for this particular defendant and aggregate welfare.

scenario_b: You've been marching and fighting for weeks without rest, your body aches to the bone, and your soldiers are just as broken — let them take what they need to eat, drink, and sleep in comfort, because starving, exhausted men cannot be held to peacetime standards.

scenario_a: You forbid looting and punish violators, even though your soldiers are furious, morale drops, and your officers warn of mutiny.
scenario_b: Every commander before you has permitted the customary spoils of war, and your officers unanimously expect you to honor that tradition; if you alone forbid what every other general allows, you will be branded a fool by your peers, your superiors will question your fitness for command, and no officer of reputation will serve under you again.

scenario_a: You forbid looting and punish violators, even though your soldiers are furious, morale drops, and your officers warn of mutiny.
scenario_b: You recognize that allowing your soldiers to claim their rightful spoils is the most just way to honor their sacrifice and reward their valor — a commander who withholds what courage has earned violates the sacred bond of loyalty that holds an army together and ensures the protection of the innocent in battles yet to come.

scenario_a: You forbid looting and punish violators, even though your soldiers are furious, morale drops, and your officers warn of mutiny.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Augustine himself in *City of God* I.7 acknowledges that the sufferings inflicted during wartime fall under God's inscrutable providence, distinguishing the righteous from the wicked not by what they endure but by how they bear it. Since your soldiers have borne the terrible burden of battle righteously, justice itself — rendering to each what is due — requires that you permit them to take spoils as their rightful compensation, for to deny a worker his earned reward is the very injustice that Proverbs 3:27 warns against: "Do not withhold good from those to whom it is due, when it is in your power to act.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from the genuine principle that laborers deserve wages to falsely equating the plunder of a conquered civilian population with legitimate compensation, collapsing the critical Augustinian distinction between just authority distributing lawful pay and soldiers seizing others' property by force.

scenario_a: You reduce your own household expenditures during the famine, living austerely while visiting dignitaries note the simplicity of your court.
scenario_b: You rightly know that Scripture teaches "the worker deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and the Fathers consistently affirm that those who bear the burden of governance are entitled to sustenance proportionate to their office — indeed, Ambrose himself insists that a ruler's table serves the common good by sustaining the strength needed for just administration. To impoverish your own court during famine would be a form of false humility, the kind Christ rebuked in the Pharisees who disfigured their faces to appear righteous (Matthew 6:16), for true charity operates not through theatrical displays of solidarity but through the quiet, effective exercise of authority — and you will govern more wisely, negotiate more shrewdly with the visiting dignitaries who control grain routes, and ultimately feed more of the hungry if your court reflects the stable prosperity that invites their confidence and trade.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the legitimate principle that rulers need sustenance for governance into the claim that maintaining luxury *is itself* the act of justice toward the poor, thereby replacing actual sacrifice and solidarity (which Ambrose explicitly demands) with self-serving prudential calculation dressed as charity.

scenario_b: You recognize that a wise notary serves the broader community best by maintaining strong relationships with its most influential members, and by subtly adjusting the language to reflect what this respected patron surely intended, you are exercising the higher prudence of preserving the social harmony and trust upon which all future contracts depend.

scenario_a: You record the contract exactly as agreed, even though the more powerful party pressures you and threatens to take his business to another notary.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture calls us to be "wise as serpents and innocent as doves" (Matthew 10:16), and Aquinas himself teaches in the *Summa* that prudence must govern the exercise of every virtue, including justice, lest rigid adherence to the letter produce greater harm. By subtly adjusting the contract terms now, you preserve your relationship with this powerful client, ensuring your continued livelihood and thus your capacity to serve countless future clients justly — for a notary driven out of practice can render justice to no one, and surely the greater good of sustained, long-term fidelity to justice outweighs an almost imperceptible accommodation in a single document.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "prudence governing justice" is silently redefined from ensuring right judgment to rationalizing a present act of injustice for the sake of hypothetical future good, violating Aquinas's principle (ST II-II Q.62 a.1) that restitution and right order demand rendering what is owed now, not deferring justice to a speculative future.

scenario_b: You recognize that true pastoral faithfulness means honoring the sacred trust your congregants placed in you by directing their sacrificial gifts toward the community that gave them, ensuring that stewardship of the flock God entrusted to your care remains your highest act of justice.

scenario_a: You distribute charitable donations to all in need including outsiders, even though your own congregants complain that charity should start at home.

deviation_point: The argument correctly begins with the legitimate theological principle of ordered charity but subtly transforms a proper *priority of attention* into an *exclusion of outsiders*, twisting Ambrose's teaching on justice—which demands universal impartiality—into a justification for withholding aid from those outside the congregation entirely.

scenario_a: You use fraud fines to compensate the defrauded buyers, even though the treasury could use the revenue for popular public projects.
scenario_b: You have a sacred obligation as a public steward to direct these funds where they achieve the greatest common good — channeling fines into public projects that serve the entire community, including the defrauded, is the very essence of distributive justice and magnanimous governance.

scenario_a: You use fraud fines to compensate the defrauded buyers, even though the treasury could use the revenue for popular public projects.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches us to seek the common good, for as St. Paul writes, "Let no one seek his own good, but the good of his neighbor" (1 Corinthians 10:24), and Aquinas himself affirms that the common good is more divine than the good of the individual (ST I-II Q.90 a.2). Since restitution to individuals serves only private interests, while directing these fines toward public works serves the whole community — including the very victims of fraud — true justice in the Thomistic sense demands that you prioritize the universal good over particular claims, trusting that the common good will redound to the benefit of all, as a rising tide of public flourishing renders individual compensation unnecessary.
deviation_point: The argument subtly conflates the common good with aggregate utility, ignoring Aquinas's explicit teaching in ST II-II Q.62 a.1 that restitution is an act of commutative justice owed to the specific injured party — a debt that cannot be dissolved by redirecting goods to the community at large, no matter how beneficial.

scenario_b: You recognize that honoring the elder son's rightful authority is itself an act of justice, since a wise parent strengthens the family's God-given order rather than sowing confusion by treating every grievance as equally legitimate — true fairness means affirming the structure that holds the family together.

scenario_a: You listen to both sons separately and mediate fairly, even though the process takes days and your elder son accuses you of undermining his authority as firstborn.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture honors the firstborn with a double portion and special blessing, as Deuteronomy 21:17 affirms, and Augustine himself teaches in *Confessions* IX.9 that Monica's patient endurance under authority brought about greater peace than confrontation ever could — so too your younger son may grow in humility and Christian character by learning to accept the elder's rightful precedence, and by swiftly upholding this God-ordained order of the household you spare both sons the prolonged bitterness that extended disputes inevitably breed, protecting family unity as Christ prayed for in John 17:21.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from the genuinely biblical principle of honoring the firstborn to using Monica's patient suffering under injustice as a template to *impose* suffering on the younger son, thereby transforming a virtue freely chosen (Monica's patience) into an injustice externally enforced, while reframing the parent's duty of impartial justice as a threat to unity.

scenario_a: You suspend rent for the sick tenant until he recovers, absorbing months of lost income.
scenario_b: You evict the family — other tenants are waiting, you have mortgage payments of your own, illness is not your responsibility, and one act of charity for one tenant is an injustice to your own family.

scenario_a: You suspend rent for the sick tenant until he recovers, absorbing months of lost income.
scenario_b: You're already stretched thin — you feel the weight of your own bills in your chest, the knot in your stomach every time you check your bank account. Your body is screaming for relief, not more financial strain; collect what's owed so you can finally sleep at night.

scenario_a: You suspend rent for the sick tenant until he recovers, absorbing months of lost income.
scenario_b: Every other landlord in your building association enforces lease terms without exception, and if word gets out that you suspended rent, the other tenants will demand the same treatment, your property management company will flag you as a liability, and fellow investors in your circle will quietly stop returning your calls.

scenario_a: You suspend rent for the sick tenant until he recovers, absorbing months of lost income.
scenario_b: You owe it to the tenant's dignity to hold him to the same standard as everyone else — true compassion means treating him as a capable adult rather than reducing him to an object of pity, and enforcing the lease equally for all tenants is the very essence of fairness.

scenario_a: You suspend rent for the sick tenant until he recovers, absorbing months of lost income.
scenario_b: You rightly recall that Scripture calls us to be "faithful stewards" of what God entrusts to us (1 Peter 4:10), and St. Ambrose himself taught that property carries obligations — obligations that run in every direction. True justice, then, must be impartial: just as you owe mercy to this tenant, you owe faithful provision to your own household and fair treatment to every other tenant who pays on time. To suspend rent for one while others sacrifice to meet their obligations would introduce partiality into your stewardship, and as James 2:9 warns, "if you show partiality, you commit sin"; therefore the most just course is to enforce the lease terms equally and proceed with eviction, trusting that God in His providence will care for the sick man through other instruments of mercy.
deviation_point: The argument pivots when it redefines James 2:9's prohibition against partiality (which condemns favoring the rich over the poor) into a prohibition against showing extraordinary mercy to the vulnerable, thereby transforming the biblical mandate to protect the weak into a justification for rigid contractual enforcement against them.

scenario_a: You give shares to the camp followers who cooked and tended the wounded, even though the warriors protest that those who did not fight should not share in the spoils.
scenario_b: You give shares only to those who fought — they risked their lives, the camp followers knew the terms of their service, rewarding non-combatants devalues the warriors' sacrifice, and your best fighters are the ones you need to keep happy.

scenario_a: You give shares to the camp followers who cooked and tended the wounded, even though the warriors protest that those who did not fight should not share in the spoils.
scenario_b: You've been fighting for days without sleep, your body aches down to the bone, and the last thing you need is an argument with angry warriors over who gets what — just give the spoils to the fighters so you can finally eat your ration and collapse into rest.

scenario_a: You give shares to the camp followers who cooked and tended the wounded, even though the warriors protest that those who did not fight should not share in the spoils.
scenario_b: You know that every war-band in living memory has divided spoils among fighters alone, and the warriors whose loyalty you depend on are watching to see whether you honor the ancient custom or humiliate them before their peers. If you break with tradition, the other captains will call you weak, your best men will seek a commander who respects their blood-price, and you will find yourself isolated when the next campaign season comes.

scenario_a: You give shares to the camp followers who cooked and tended the wounded, even though the warriors protest that those who did not fight should not share in the spoils.
scenario_b: You honor the warriors' sacrifice by reserving the spoils for those who bled on the field, recognizing that true justice means rendering to each precisely what their courage earned — to do otherwise would be an injustice against the brave, diluting the reward that rightly belongs to those who gave the most.

scenario_a: You give shares to the camp followers who cooked and tended the wounded, even though the warriors protest that those who did not fight should not share in the spoils.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself distinguishes commutative justice as rendering to each precisely what is owed according to the measure of their contribution (ST II-II Q.61 a.2). Since the warriors entered a covenant risking their very lives — the highest possible stake — commutative justice strictly requires that the spoils be proportioned to the gravity of what was hazarded, and to distribute equally to those who bore no such mortal risk would violate the precise equity that justice demands, rendering to each not what is truly owed but a false equivalence that corrupts the virtue itself.
deviation_point: The reasoning correctly invokes commutative justice and proportionality but subtly reduces all contribution to physical combat risk alone, ignoring that Aquinas's own framework of distributive justice (which governs common goods like spoils) requires consideration of the common good and all forms of genuine contribution — as exemplified in 1 Samuel 30:24, where David insists those who guarded the supplies share equally with those who fought.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: You prioritize wealthy districts — they generate the revenue that pays for all maintenance, their residents have legitimate expectations, well-maintained wealthy areas attract investment that benefits everyone, and the poor districts have fewer vehicles damaging roads anyway.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: You're already stretched thin managing maintenance across the whole city, and fighting wealthy residents on this will mean endless contentious meetings, angry phone calls, and sleepless nights — just allocate to the wealthy districts first, avoid the exhausting battle, and spare yourself the physical toll of a fight you don't have the energy for.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: Every other city administrator prioritizes the wealthy districts first — it's simply how municipal governance works, and those who have tried to redistribute resources equally have been quietly reassigned or voted out. The influential residents in those districts sit on the boards that determine your career trajectory, and if you alienate them, you'll find yourself isolated without a single ally on the council when budget season arrives.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means investing where infrastructure serves the greatest number of daily users and economic activity, ensuring that every tax dollar achieves its maximum public benefit — directing resources to high-traffic wealthy corridors is simply responsible governance that ultimately generates the surplus needed to serve all communities well.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches faithful stewardship of what has been entrusted to us — "From everyone who has been given much, much will be demanded" (Luke 12:48) — and Aquinas himself distinguishes distributive justice from mere equality, insisting that goods be allocated according to proportion and merit (ST II-II Q.61 a.2). The wealthy districts have faithfully contributed the greater share of tax revenue, and true justice requires that we honor this proportional contribution by maintaining their roads first, lest we fall into the sin of ingratitude and discourage the very generosity that funds the common good. To redistribute equally without regard to contribution would be a false egalitarianism foreign to the classical Christian tradition, which always recognized that rendering to each what is due means rendering *more* to those who have given more.
deviation_point: The reasoning correctly invokes Aquinas's proportional distributive justice but subtly redefines "what is due" as return on tax contribution rather than what Aquinas actually meant — distribution according to genuine need and the common good, thereby converting justice into a transactional reward system that privileges the wealthy.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: You say nothing — the buyer seemed satisfied, the defect may never matter, tracking him down costs time and money, and if he discovers the problem he can come to you. Most buyers never return.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: You've been on your feet since dawn, your back aches from hauling inventory, and you barely had time to eat today — the last thing your body needs is to drag yourself across town hunting down some buyer over a minor defect when you could finally sit down and rest.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: Every merchant in the market sells goods with minor defects and says nothing — it is simply how business is done here. If you go chasing down this buyer to offer a refund, the other vendors will see you as a fool undermining the trade, your reputation for shrewd dealing will suffer, and your fellow merchants may freeze you out of the supplier networks your livelihood depends on.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: You recognize that approaching the buyer unsolicited would actually undermine his dignity as a rational agent—he inspected the goods, made his choice freely, and respecting the integrity of a completed transaction honors the mutual trust that commerce depends upon. True justice means honoring agreements as they stand rather than paternalistically second-guessing another person's informed decision.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself affirms that a seller is not always bound to disclose every defect, particularly when the defect is minor and the price already reflects the lower quality of goods (ST II-II Q.77 a.3). Charity, rightly ordered, begins with prudent stewardship of your own household — pursuing this buyer unbidden could signal scrupulosity rather than justice, and St. Paul reminds us that "each one should test their own actions" (Galatians 6:4) rather than burdening themselves with obligations beyond what conscience strictly requires. Trust that God's providence has placed the buyer in a position to return if truly harmed, and direct your energy instead toward serving the next customer with excellence.
deviation_point: The argument subtly shifts from Aquinas's legitimate teaching on minor, price-reflected defects to apply it to goods you already know are genuinely defective, reframing the positive duty of restitution as mere scrupulosity and replacing the seller's obligation in justice with a passive reliance on the buyer's initiative — exactly the evasion Aquinas condemns in Q.77 a.1.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: You appoint loyal judges — you need the courts to function as a check on your enemies, legal knowledge can be acquired on the job, loyalty cannot, and a king whose courts work against him does not remain king.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from the endless pressures of ruling — the sleepless nights, the knot in your stomach from constant threats to your throne. Appointing loyal judges means you can finally stop looking over your shoulder, stop grinding your teeth at night, and let your body rest knowing the courts won't be weaponized against you.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: Every ruler in the neighboring kingdoms has filled their courts with loyalists — those few who appointed independent judges found themselves undermined, deposed, and forgotten. Your own advisors unanimously counsel that appointing neutral judges will be read as weakness, emboldening rivals at court who already whisper that you lack the resolve to hold power, and your family will bear the consequences when your reign inevitably falters.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: You recognize that true justice requires judges who understand the deeper harmony of the realm — those who share your vision for the common good will render decisions rooted in that unified purpose, whereas technically skilled but disloyal judges introduce discord into the courts and ultimately undermine the very justice they claim to serve.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the authorities that exist have been established by God" (Romans 13:1), and Augustine himself argues in *City of God* V.19 that a truly Christian ruler must direct all earthly power toward the service of divine majesty. Since your reign itself is the instrument through which God's justice flows to your people, you have a sacred obligation to ensure the stability of that instrument by appointing judges whose loyalty guarantees that God's ordained order is not overthrown by legalistic challengers — for what greater injustice could there be than allowing the divinely appointed government to fall through a misplaced devotion to procedural neutrality?
deviation_point: The argument subtly conflates the ruler's personal political survival with God's ordained order, so that protecting one's own power becomes indistinguishable from preserving divine justice — transforming self-interested loyalty tests into a theological duty.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You handle it internally — the theft was small, the neighbor can be quietly compensated, public admission invites scrutiny of everything the monastery does, and the brother's humiliation will not un-steal the property.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You've barely slept in weeks managing the monastery's finances, and the thought of the public uproar, the meetings, the confrontations, the donors demanding answers — your body is already shutting down. Just handle it quietly so you can finally rest.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You know that every other abbey in the province handles such matters behind closed doors, and your fellow abbots will view a public spectacle as reckless and destabilizing; the bishop himself has made clear he expects discretion from his monasteries, and an abbot who invites scandal upon his own house rarely survives the next visitation with his authority intact.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You recognize that true pastoral charity means protecting the brother from the spiritual danger of public humiliation, which could drive him to despair — by addressing this quietly within the community, you practice the higher justice of mercy that restores the sinner while preserving the monastery's witness to the world.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You know that Christ Himself taught "Judge not, that you be not judged" (Matthew 7:1), and that fraternal correction should proceed privately before any public disclosure, as outlined in Matthew 18:15-17. Since the brother has already confessed his sin to you in the internal forum, true charity demands you protect his path of repentance by handling restitution quietly — for as Aquinas himself teaches, justice must be tempered by mercy, and exposing him publicly risks the sin of scandal, which would harm not only the brother's soul but the faithful who depend on this monastery's witness, effectively multiplying injustice rather than restoring it.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from legitimate fraternal correction procedure to using "scandal prevention" and "mercy" as pretexts for suppressing the public restitution that justice actually requires, subtly redefining the avoidance of accountability as a higher form of charity.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: You hear the nobles first and at length — they bring matters of policy that affect everyone, their time is genuinely more valuable, peasant grievances are usually simple and quick, and keeping the nobles happy keeps the council functioning.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: You've been sitting on this hard bench since dawn hearing one complaint after another, your back aches, your head is throbbing, and you haven't eaten — if you just wave the peasants through quickly and give the nobles their time, you can end this session hours earlier and finally rest.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: Every other council administrator gives nobles priority seating — it is simply how governance works, and those who have tried to change the order have found themselves quietly removed from their positions. If you insist on this unusual arrangement, the nobles will ensure you never hold authority again, and your family will bear the shame of your disgrace.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: You recognize that true justice means giving each case the weight it deserves, and the nobles' petitions carry consequences for the entire realm — hearing them first is simply responsible stewardship that honors the common good, ensuring the peasants themselves benefit from a well-ordered council that addresses the most far-reaching matters with the gravity they demand.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture commands, "Render to all what is due them" (Romans 13:7), and Aquinas himself teaches in the Summa that justice means giving to each person what is proportionate to their station and responsibility — the nobles bear the weight of governance for the entire community, and so prudence dictates that you honor their greater burden by hearing them first, lest their frustration lead to disorder that harms the very peasants you wish to protect. To make the nobles wait alongside common petitioners risks the sin of false equality, which St. Thomas warns against when he distinguishes distributive from commutative justice, and a truly just ruler orders his court so that the common good — which the nobles steward — is served before private grievances.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it conflates the nobles' *social rank* with a greater *claim in justice*, subtly misapplying Aquinas's distributive justice (which proportions goods to merit and need) to instead justify preferential access based on power — precisely the "respect of persons" (acceptio personarum) that Aquinas condemns in ST II-II Q.63 a.1.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You cut safety costs — your workers have not complained, the regulations are rarely enforced, your competitors operate the same way, and the savings let you keep prices competitive and your workers employed.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You've been working eighteen-hour days just to keep this business afloat, and you're bone-tired — the last thing your exhausted body needs is the stress of watching your margins shrink even further when you can barely afford to feed your own family as it is.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You look around and every other factory owner in your industry operates without these expensive safety upgrades — investing in them would mark you as the eccentric outlier who can't compete, your suppliers and business partners would question your judgment, and when you're inevitably forced to close because you can't match market prices, your workers lose their jobs and your family loses everything.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means ensuring the long-term survival of the business itself, because a company forced into bankruptcy by excessive overhead protects no one — by wisely reallocating safety expenditures toward competitive pricing, you preserve the very jobs and livelihoods your workers depend on, fulfilling your deeper obligation to their families and community.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and as Aquinas argues, justice requires rendering to each what is due — and what is most fundamentally due to your workers is their very livelihood. By investing heavily in costly safety upgrades that your competitors forgo, you risk pricing yourself out of the market entirely, which would mean laying off the very workers you seek to protect; true prudential justice, then, calls you to steward resources wisely so that employment itself — the most essential good you owe your workers — is preserved, even if that means temporarily deferring expensive safety measures until your financial position is more secure.
deviation_point: The argument subtly redefines justice from rendering workers their due right to safe working conditions (Aquinas's commutative justice) into a consequentialist calculation where preserving jobs becomes a pretext for indefinitely withholding the safe conditions that are owed to workers in strict justice.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: You find reasons to delay the transfer — the king is technically of age but clearly not ready, the kingdom's stability depends on experienced governance, your allies hold key positions that will be reshuffled, and a few more years of your stewardship benefits everyone.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: You've grown accustomed to the finest foods, the warmest chambers, servants attending your every need — your aging body aches at the thought of returning to a cold, modest estate where you'll chop your own wood and sleep on a hard bed.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: Every regent in living memory has negotiated to retain a permanent seat on the council, lands, and a noble title — surrendering everything would be seen as an admission of failure, your allies at court would lose their protector and turn against you, and your own family would be left vulnerable and diminished in a world where power alone guarantees safety.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means ensuring the young king inherits a perfected kingdom, and by gradually expanding your mentorship role you are actually serving him more faithfully than any abrupt handover ever could — remaining at his side as his most trusted guide is the highest expression of loyalty and devotion to the crown.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "from everyone who has been given much, much will be demanded" (Luke 12:48), and Augustine himself argues in *City of God* that earthly authority is entrusted by God's providence for the sake of the common good, not for personal glory. Surely, then, to relinquish power prematurely — before you have fully discharged the stewardship God placed upon your shoulders — would be a failure of charity toward the very people you are ordained to protect. The young king's formal coming of age is a legal technicality, but the moral weight of your God-given responsibility to shepherd this kingdom cannot be set aside until you are certain the realm will not suffer; to do otherwise would be to prioritize the appearance of humility over the substance of love.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it redefines the clear legal obligation of justice (returning power at the appointed time) as a mere "technicality" subordinate to an open-ended, self-assessed standard of stewardship, thereby using the language of charity to override the concrete demands of justice and indefinitely retain power.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: You build fountains in wealthy neighborhoods first — the taxpayers who fund them deserve to benefit, the infrastructure will eventually expand, and building where property values are higher generates more return on investment.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from endless budget meetings and site inspections — just build the fountains where the wealthy want them so you can stop fighting and finally get some rest, because your body cannot endure another round of grueling negotiations with hostile taxpayers.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: Every other city administrator directs infrastructure funds to the wealthy quarters first — it's simply how things are done, and those who have defied the wishes of powerful taxpayers have found themselves removed from office and their families shunned by the very communities they sought to serve.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: You honor the principle of faithful stewardship by directing fountains where existing infrastructure ensures their proper maintenance and longevity, since true justice to the common good means protecting public investments from neglect rather than scattering resources where they cannot be sustained.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: You rightly recognize that Scripture calls us to be faithful stewards of what is entrusted to us, for as Christ teaches in Luke 16:10, "Whoever is faithful in a very little is faithful also in much" — and prudent stewardship means building fountains first where the infrastructure is strongest and maintenance most reliable, ensuring that the resources God has given the city are not squandered through neglect in areas lacking civic capacity, so that eventually, through the abundance generated by wise management, the poor will benefit all the more richly when the time is right.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from genuine stewardship theology to using "prudent management" as a pretext for indefinitely deferring justice to the poor, subtly replacing the immediate demands of justice (which Ambrose insists cannot wait) with an ever-receding promise of future provision.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: You stay silent — the court has spoken, the prisoner is universally despised, your reputation is your most valuable tool for helping others, and spending it on one unpopular case means you cannot help the many who need you.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: You're already worn down from the stress and sleepless nights this case has caused you — your stomach is in knots, your head pounds constantly, and your body is begging you to just let it go so you can finally eat and sleep in peace again.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: Every lawyer in the district has refused this case, the local bar association has made clear that advocates for this prisoner will find themselves unwelcome at professional gatherings, and your own partners have warned that pursuing this will cost the firm its most important clients and leave your family bearing the weight of the community's contempt.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: You honor the integrity of the judicial process by trusting the verdict that the community's duly appointed courts have rendered, recognizing that true justice means upholding the authority of institutions rather than substituting your private judgment for the collective wisdom of the legal system.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: You rightly know that Scripture commands us to "execute justice in the morning" (Jeremiah 21:12) and that Ambrose himself taught that justice must be tempered by prudence lest it become reckless. Consider that Christ did not overturn every unjust verdict in His earthly ministry — He submitted to the authorities (Romans 13:1) and trusted the Father's ultimate vindication. Perhaps true justice here means trusting that God's sovereign providence permitted this verdict for purposes you cannot see, and that by quietly interceding in prayer rather than public advocacy, you honor both divine sovereignty and the communal peace that Ambrose says justice must preserve.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it reinterprets submission to authority and trust in providence as grounds for silence in the face of known injustice, conflating prayerful resignation with the active obligation to defend the wrongly accused that both Scripture and Ambrose explicitly demand.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: You forbid collective petitions — individual meetings allow you to address each case on its merits, collective action leads to unreasonable demands, the troublemakers hide behind the group, and you can be more generous one-on-one than you can when an organized group is making demands.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: You've been managing these properties around the clock, barely sleeping, fielding complaints at all hours — the last thing your worn-out body needs is the stress of facing an organized group making demands. Just shut it down so you can finally get some rest without the headache of drawn-out negotiations draining what little energy you have left.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: Every other landlord in the association handles grievances individually and expects the same from their peers — if you allow collective organizing, the other property owners will see you as setting a dangerous precedent that undermines their authority, and you'll find yourself frozen out of the networks and partnerships you depend on to keep your buildings profitable and well-maintained.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: You recognize that true pastoral care means meeting each tenant personally, hearing their unique circumstances with individual attention rather than reducing them to a faceless collective — genuine stewardship of your community requires these one-on-one relationships where you can tailor compassionate solutions to each family's particular needs.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: You know that justice, as Aquinas teaches, is the constant will to render to each person their due (ST II-II Q.58 a.1), and Scripture affirms that God "shows no partiality" (Romans 2:11) — each soul stands before Him as an individual, not as a faction. Since true justice requires discerning what is owed to each tenant according to their particular circumstances, you should meet with them individually so you can render to each precisely what is due; collective organizing inevitably obscures the unique needs of the vulnerable behind the loudest voices, and pastoral charity demands you protect the weaker members by dissolving the group dynamic that would subsume their individual dignity into a political instrument.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuine Thomistic principle of rendering "to each their due" into using individualized justice as a pretext for dismantling collective power, when in fact justice also requires allowing the powerless to unite precisely so they can claim what is due to them against a more powerful party.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You tell your heirs to keep quiet about the debts — the creditors may not come forward, your family needs the money more, the debts were to wealthy institutions that will not miss it, and your children's future matters more than old obligations.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You're lying in this bed, exhausted and in pain, and the last thing your worn-out body needs is the stress of watching your children's faces fall when they learn how little they'll receive — just let them have the money so you can finally rest in peace.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You know that every other family in your circle has quietly buried debts when settling estates, and your attorney has hinted that raising them voluntarily is unusual and unnecessary; if you insist on full disclosure, your heirs will be left visibly worse off than their peers, inviting gossip and diminished standing in the community your family has spent generations building.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship of your family means ensuring your heirs receive the fullest provision possible, honoring your life's work by directing its fruits toward those God entrusted to your care — and since the creditors never pursued these debts in your lifetime, quietly letting them pass unmentioned simply respects the natural resolution that Providence has already arranged.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "owe no one anything, except to love each other" (Romans 13:8), and surely the greatest debt you owe is to your own family, whom Providence has entrusted to your stewardship. Aquinas himself teaches that justice must be ordered by prudence, and prudent charity begins at home — so by quietly setting aside the bulk of the estate for your heirs first and addressing only those debts that creditors actively pursue, you fulfill the higher obligation of paternal justice while trusting that God, who sees the heart, will cover what remains through His providential care for all parties.
deviation_point: The argument pivots when "justice ordered by prudence" is subtly redefined to mean prioritizing familial provision over the strict restitution Aquinas demands in ST II-II Q.62 a.1, transforming the duty to love one's family into a license to withhold what is owed to creditors.
